statement: Helium was first detected in the Sun before it was found on Earth — it is named after Helios, the Greek god of the Sun.
quizQuestion: Helium was discovered in the Sun before Earth. Which Greek deity is it named after?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, helium was first detected as an unknown spectral line in sunlight during a solar eclipse in 1868. Norman Lockyer proposed it was a new element and named it after Helios, the Greek sun god. It wasn't found on Earth until 1895.
wowFactor: Helium was discovered in the Sun 27 years before anyone found it on Earth — astronomers spotted an unknown element in the Sun's spectrum in 1868, and named it after the Sun god. It's literally a piece of starlight!

variants.question: True or false: helium was first detected in the Sun before being found on Earth.

statement: Lithium is the least dense metal and the least dense solid element — it is so light it floats on water and even on oil.
quizQuestion: Which metal is the least dense and lightest solid element?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, lithium has a density of only 0.534 g/cm³ (from Wikidata), making it the least dense metal and the least dense solid element. It is so light that it floats on water, mineral oil, and even some other light liquids.
wowFactor: Lithium is so incredibly light that a chunk of it actually floats on water — the least dense metal in existence, yet it powers the batteries in your phone!

variants.question: True or false: lithium is less dense than water and will float if placed in it.

statement: The transmutation of lithium atoms to helium in 1932 was the first fully human-made nuclear reaction in history.
quizQuestion: In 1932, which element's atoms were split to create the first fully human-made nuclear reaction?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the transmutation of lithium atoms to helium in 1932 was the first fully human-made nuclear reaction. This was achieved by John Cockcroft and Ernest Walton at the Cavendish Laboratory, winning them the Nobel Prize in Physics.
wowFactor: The first nuclear reaction ever deliberately created by humans in 1932 used lithium — the same lightweight metal you charge your phone with every night!

variants.question: True or false: the first human-made nuclear reaction in 1932 converted lithium atoms into helium.

statement: Many of the world's most distinctive smells — skunk spray, garlic, bad breath, and rotten eggs — are all caused by sulfur-containing organic compounds.
quizQuestion: Which element is responsible for the smells of skunk spray, garlic, and rotten eggs?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, many sulfur compounds are odoriferous — the smells of skunk scent, bad breath, grapefruit, and garlic are all due to organosulfur compounds. Hydrogen sulfide gives the characteristic odor to rotten eggs and biological decay processes.
wowFactor: If you've ever smelled a skunk, garlic, or rotten eggs — congratulations, you've experienced sulfur chemistry! One element is responsible for nature's most unforgettable smells!

statement: Elemental sulfur naturally forms cyclic molecules of eight atoms (S₈), making it one of the few elements that forms ring-shaped molecules at room temperature.
quizQuestion: How many sulfur atoms bond together to form elemental sulfur's natural ring-shaped molecules?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, under normal conditions sulfur atoms form cyclic octatomic molecules with the chemical formula S₈. This ring structure is why pure sulfur is a bright yellow crystalline solid — an unusual and striking appearance for a non-metallic element.
wowFactor: Pure sulfur atoms spontaneously snap together into 8-atom rings at room temperature — nature's little molecular crowns, all bright yellow and crystalline!

variants.question: True or false: elemental sulfur is a bright yellow crystalline solid at room temperature.

statement: Lead (atomic number 82) is the element with the highest atomic number among all stable elements — every element heavier than lead is radioactive.
quizQuestion: Which element has the highest atomic number while still being stable (non-radioactive)?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, lead has the highest atomic number of any stable element. Its three isotopes are endpoints of major nuclear decay chains of heavier elements, making it the 'final destination' of radioactive decay for much of the periodic table.
wowFactor: Lead is the end of the line for radioactive elements — every element heavier than lead will eventually decay and turn into lead over millions of years!

statement: The word 'plumber' comes from the Latin 'plumbum' meaning lead, because ancient Roman plumbers made water pipes from lead.
quizQuestion: What does the word 'plumber' literally derive from, based on what Roman water pipes were made of?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, lead's chemical symbol Pb comes from the Latin word 'plumbum.' The word 'plumber' derives from this, since Roman plumbers used lead extensively for water pipes. Romans were later aware of lead's toxicity but continued using it.
wowFactor: Every time you call a plumber, you're using a word that means 'lead worker' — Romans built their water system from lead pipes and named the profession after the metal!

statement: Tin has 10 stable isotopes — more than any other element in the periodic table, due to its 'magic number' of protons.
quizQuestion: Which element has the most stable isotopes of any element, with 10 stable forms?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, tin has 10 stable isotopes — the largest number of stable isotopes of any element in the periodic table. This is due to tin's 'magic number' of 50 protons, which creates an unusually stable nuclear configuration.
wowFactor: Tin wins the periodic table's stability contest — with 10 stable isotopes, it has more stable forms than any other element, thanks to its 'magic number' of protons!

statement: The Bronze Age is named after bronze — an alloy of 87.5% copper and 12.5% tin — used from as early as 3000 BC.
quizQuestion: What percentage of a traditional bronze alloy is tin?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the first tin alloy used on a large scale was bronze, made of 1/8 tin (12.5%) and 7/8 copper (87.5%), used from as early as 3000 BC. This alloy was harder than pure copper, transforming ancient toolmaking and weaponry.
wowFactor: The entire Bronze Age — one of humanity's defining eras — is named after an alloy that's mostly copper with just a small bit of tin mixed in!

statement: Mercury is the only metal that is liquid at standard room temperature and pressure, with a melting point of -38.83°C.
quizQuestion: Which metal is liquid at standard room temperature?
explanation: According to Wikidata, mercury melts at -38.83°C, meaning it is always liquid at room temperature. It is the only metallic element that is liquid at standard conditions — making it uniquely useful historically in thermometers, barometers, and mirrors.
wowFactor: Mercury is the only metal that's liquid at room temperature — pour it out and it rolls around like liquid silver, the strangest metal you'll ever see!

statement: Sodium ions rushing through voltage-gated channels enable nerve impulses — every thought you have involves sodium atoms moving in and out of your neurons.
quizQuestion: What process uses sudden sodium ion flow through channels to transmit nerve signals?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in nerve cells the sudden flow of sodium ions into the cell through voltage-gated sodium channels enables transmission of a nerve impulse in a process called the action potential. Animal cells use the sodium-potassium pump to maintain sodium concentration differences.
wowFactor: Every single thought you've ever had was powered by sodium — millions of tiny sodium atoms rushing through your neurons are literally what thinking feels like at the atomic level!

statement: Sodium is the sixth most abundant element in Earth's crust, and together with chlorine forms common table salt (NaCl).
quizQuestion: What is sodium's rank among the most abundant elements in Earth's crust?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, sodium is the sixth most abundant element in Earth's crust and exists in minerals like feldspars and halite (NaCl). Sodium and chlorine are also the most common dissolved elements by weight in the world's oceans.
wowFactor: Sodium is so common in Earth's crust that you're literally standing on sodium right now — and every pinch of table salt contains sodium bonded to chlorine at a 1-to-1 atomic ratio!

statement: Beryllium is the most common window material for X-ray equipment because its low density makes it nearly transparent to X-rays while being structurally strong.
quizQuestion: Which element is used as the window material in X-ray equipment because it is nearly transparent to X-rays?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, because of its low density and atomic mass, beryllium is relatively transparent to X-rays and other forms of ionizing radiation — making it the most common window material for X-ray equipment and components of particle detectors. Its density is just 1.85 times that of water.
wowFactor: Beryllium is nearly invisible to X-rays — that's why every medical X-ray machine uses a beryllium window. The metal that lets radiation pass through is the unsung hero of medical imaging!

statement: Beryllium copper alloy tools will not create sparks when struck against steel — making them essential safety tools in oil refineries and other explosive environments.
quizQuestion: Which alloy is used to make tools that cannot create sparks when struck against steel?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, tools made of beryllium copper alloys are strong, hard, and do not create sparks when they strike a steel surface — making them essential in environments where a single spark could ignite flammable gas or dust, such as oil refineries, grain elevators, and munitions factories.
wowFactor: Beryllium copper tools are used in oil refineries and explosive factories because — uniquely among metals — they literally cannot make sparks. One invisible property saves countless lives!

statement: Calcium is the fifth most abundant element in Earth's crust and the most abundant metal in the human body, essential for bones, teeth, and nerve function.
quizQuestion: What rank does calcium hold among the most abundant metals in the human body?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, calcium is the most abundant metal and the fifth-most abundant element in the human body. It plays vital roles in signal transduction, neurotransmitter release, muscle contraction, and bone and tooth formation.
wowFactor: Calcium is the most abundant metal in your entire body — your skeleton is essentially a calcium storage system that also holds you upright!

statement: Neon is the fifth most abundant element in the entire universe, yet is comparatively scarce on Earth because it cannot form compounds and escaped from the early planet.
quizQuestion: What rank does neon hold in cosmic abundance in the universe?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neon's synthesis in the cosmos resulted from nuclear fusion within stars, ranking it fifth in cosmic abundance after hydrogen, helium, oxygen, and carbon. Despite this universal abundance, neon is comparatively scarce on Earth because its high volatility and inability to form compounds prevented it from being retained.
wowFactor: Neon is everywhere in the universe — the fifth most abundant element after hydrogen, helium, oxygen, and carbon — yet Earth has almost none of it because neon refuses to form compounds and just floated away!

statement: Neon's distinctive bright red emission spectrum was immediately recognizable when it was discovered in 1898, and still gives neon signs their characteristic red-orange glow today.
quizQuestion: What color does neon emit when electrically excited in a discharge tube?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neon's discovery was marked by the distinctive bright red emission spectrum it exhibited, leading to its immediate recognition as a new element. This same emission spectrum gives neon signs their characteristic reddish-orange glow and is responsible for the red light in helium-neon lasers.
wowFactor: When scientists discovered neon in 1898, its glowing red-orange light was so unmistakably unique they instantly knew it was a new element — and that same red glow is in every neon sign ever made!

statement: Zinc is the only metal that appears in all six classes of enzymes — making it uniquely essential to every major type of biochemical reaction in living organisms.
quizQuestion: Which essential trace metal is the only one found in all six enzyme classes?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, zinc is the only metal which appears in all enzyme classes. It is the second most abundant trace metal in humans after iron, an important cofactor for many enzymes, and essential for prenatal and postnatal development.
wowFactor: Zinc is the biochemist's Swiss army knife — it's the only metal found in every single category of enzyme, meaning no type of biological reaction can happen everywhere without zinc!

variants.question: True or false: zinc is the only metal that appears in all six classes of enzymes.

statement: Brass — an alloy of copper and zinc — was used as early as the third millennium BC in the Aegean area, thousands of years before zinc was identified as a separate element.
quizQuestion: Which copper-zinc alloy has been used by humans since at least the third millennium BC?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, brass — an alloy of copper and zinc in various proportions — was used as early as the third millennium BC in the Aegean area and modern Iraq, UAE, and Georgia. Zinc metal itself was not produced on a large scale until the 12th century in India, meaning people used the alloy for thousands of years without knowing zinc as an element.
wowFactor: Humans made brass — a copper-zinc alloy — for over 4,000 years before anyone knew zinc was even a separate element. They were doing alloy metallurgy without understanding the chemistry!

statement: Magnesium is produced inside large aging stars by fusing three helium nuclei onto carbon, then scattered across the universe when those stars explode as supernovas.
quizQuestion: How is magnesium produced inside large aging stars?
acceptableAnswers: sequential addition of helium nuclei to carbon
explanation: According to Wikipedia, magnesium is produced in large, aging stars by the sequential addition of three helium nuclei to a carbon nucleus. When such stars explode as supernovas, much of the magnesium is expelled into the interstellar medium — which is why Earth contains magnesium at all.
wowFactor: Every magnesium atom on Earth was forged inside a dying star and blasted across space in a supernova explosion — the magnesium in your bones was created before our solar system existed!

variants.question: True or false: magnesium atoms on Earth were originally created inside stars that later exploded as supernovas.

statement: Phosphorus was first isolated in 1669 by Hennig Brand, who discovered it by accident while evaporating large quantities of urine in search of gold.
quizQuestion: How did Hennig Brand accidentally discover phosphorus in 1669?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, phosphorus was isolated by Hennig Brand in 1669, marking the scientific community's first discovery of an element since antiquity. Brand isolated it from urine while searching for the Philosopher's Stone — the glowing material he found was white phosphorus, which emits faint light when exposed to oxygen.
wowFactor: Phosphorus was discovered by a man who boiled 60 buckets of urine looking for gold — instead he found a glowing substance that became one of the most important elements in biology and modern agriculture!

variants.question: True or false: phosphorus was the first element discovered since antiquity, isolated in 1669.

statement: The potassium-40 isotope found in natural potassium is radioactive — making it the most common radioisotope in the human body, present in every banana and your own muscles.
quizQuestion: Which radioactive isotope is the most common radioisotope found in the human body?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, naturally occurring potassium is composed of three isotopes, of which potassium-40 (40K) is radioactive. Traces of 40K are found in all natural sources of potassium — making it the most common radioisotope in the human body. Bananas are famous for containing potassium and therefore tiny amounts of this radioactivity.
wowFactor: You are radioactive right now! Potassium-40 in your muscles is the most common radioisotope in the human body — and eating a banana literally adds a tiny measurable amount of radioactivity to you!

variants.question: True or false: potassium-40 is radioactive and is the most common radioisotope in the human body.

statement: Fluorine is the most reactive element, reacting with all other elements except the lightest noble gases.
quizQuestion: Which chemical element reacts with every other element except the lightest noble gases?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, fluorine is extremely reactive as it reacts with all other elements except for the light noble gases. Wikidata confirms it has the highest electronegativity of any element at 3.98, making it the strongest oxidising agent known.
wowFactor: Fluorine is so aggressive it reacts with glass, water, and even some noble gases — scientists had to invent special containers just to store it safely.

variants.question: True or false: fluorine reacts with every element except the lightest noble gases.

statement: Fluorine compounds include PTFE (Teflon), refrigerants, toothpaste fluoride, and pharmaceuticals like Prozac and Lipitor.
quizQuestion: Which element is found in both Teflon and the fluoride in your toothpaste?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, organofluorine compounds are widely used as refrigerants, electrical insulation, and PTFE (Teflon). Pharmaceuticals such as atorvastatin (Lipitor) and fluoxetine (Prozac) contain carbon-fluorine bonds. The fluoride ion inhibits dental cavities and is used in toothpaste and water fluoridation.
wowFactor: The same scary reactive element that dissolves glass is also in your toothpaste every morning and in the non-stick pan you cook with — fluorine is everywhere in disguise.

variants.question: True or false: the active ingredient in Prozac (fluoxetine) contains a fluorine-carbon bond.

visualDescription: A pixel-art collage: a toothbrush, a frying pan, an antidepressant pill, and a refrigerator, all connected by glowing fluorine atoms in a neon web.

statement: Fluorine was first isolated by French chemist Henri Moissan in 1886 using low-temperature electrolysis — industrial production for uranium enrichment began during the Manhattan Project.
quizQuestion: Who first isolated elemental fluorine in 1886?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, fluorine proved difficult and dangerous to separate from its compounds, and several early experimenters died or sustained injuries from their attempts. Only in 1886 did French chemist Henri Moissan isolate elemental fluorine using low-temperature electrolysis, a process still employed for modern production. Industrial production began during the Manhattan Project for uranium enrichment.
wowFactor: Several chemists died trying to isolate fluorine before Moissan succeeded in 1886 — it was so dangerous that the race to isolate it was nicknamed 'the fluorine martyrs'.

variants.question: True or false: industrial fluorine production for uranium enrichment began during the Manhattan Project.

Fluorine was proposed as an element in ___, but was not isolated until 1886.

visualDescription: A pixel-art 1886 Paris laboratory, Henri Moissan carefully manipulating a glowing electrolysis apparatus with thick gloves, previous failed attempts chalked on the wall behind him.

statement: Argon makes up 0.934% of Earth's atmosphere — 23 times more abundant than CO2 and more than 500 times more abundant than neon.
quizQuestion: Argon is how many times more abundant in the atmosphere than carbon dioxide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, argon is the third most abundant gas in Earth's atmosphere at 0.934%. It is more than twice as abundant as water vapor, 23 times as abundant as carbon dioxide (400 ppmv), and more than 500 times as abundant as neon (18 ppmv).
wowFactor: There is 23 times more argon in the air you breathe than CO2 — yet nobody talks about argon because it does absolutely nothing, making it the world's most ignored gas.

variants.question: True or false: argon is more than 500 times more abundant in the atmosphere than neon.
Which noble gas makes up 0.934% of Earth's atmosphere, making it the most abundant noble gas?
Argon's name derives from a Greek word meaning '___', reflecting its chemical inertness.

visualDescription: A pixel-art cross-section of Earth's atmosphere with glowing layers labeled, argon shown as a thick abundant band dwarfing a tiny CO2 sliver.

statement: Argon's name comes from the Ancient Greek word for 'lazy' or 'inactive', because it undergoes almost no chemical reactions.
quizQuestion: Argon's name derives from the Greek word for which quality?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the name 'argon' derives from the Ancient Greek word ἀργόν, meaning 'lazy' or 'inactive', referring to the fact that the element undergoes almost no chemical reactions. Its complete outer electron shell makes it stable and resistant to bonding with other elements.
wowFactor: Scientists named an entire element after laziness — and it's one of the most abundant gases in your lungs right now, just doing absolutely nothing.

variants.question: True or false: the name 'argon' comes from a Greek word meaning 'lazy' or 'inactive'.

statement: Iodine deficiency affects about two billion people worldwide and is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disabilities.
quizQuestion: What is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disabilities worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, iodine is required for the synthesis of thyroid hormones, and iodine deficiency affects about two billion people and is the leading preventable cause of intellectual disabilities. Iodine is on the WHO's List of Essential Medicines.
wowFactor: Adding a tiny amount of iodine to table salt — a change costing almost nothing — has prevented intellectual disability in hundreds of millions of children worldwide.

variants.question: True or false: iodine is required for the synthesis of thyroid hormones.
Deficiency of which element affects two billion people and is the top preventable cause of intellectual disability?
Iodine was discovered by French chemist Bernard Courtois in ___.

statement: Iodine is a solid at room temperature that melts into a deep violet liquid at 113.7°C and boils into a violet gas — its name comes from the Greek word for violet.
quizQuestion: What color gas does iodine produce when it evaporates?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, iodine exists at standard conditions as a semi-lustrous, non-metallic solid that melts to form a deep violet liquid at 113.7°C and boils to a violet gas at 184.4°C. It was named by Joseph Louis Gay-Lussac after the Ancient Greek Ιώδης meaning 'violet'.
wowFactor: Iodine is named after the Greek word for violet because — uniquely among solid elements — it sublimes into a stunning purple cloud of gas that looks almost magical.

variants.question: True or false: iodine's name comes from the Greek word for 'violet', describing the color of its gas.

visualDescription: A pixel-art chemistry lab where a dark crystalline solid in a flask is gently heated and blooms into a gorgeous swirling violet cloud, the scientist watching in wonder.

statement: The electron was discovered by J. J. Thomson in 1897 at the Cavendish Laboratory and named after the Greek word for amber.
quizQuestion: Who discovered the electron in 1897?
explanation: According to Wikidata, the electron was discovered by J. J. Thomson in 1897 at the Cavendish Laboratory of Experimental Physics. According to Wikipedia, the electron is an elementary particle with a negative electric charge that makes up ordinary matter along with quarks. Its name comes from the Greek word for amber.
wowFactor: The electron's name comes from the Greek word for 'amber' — because ancient Greeks noticed that rubbing amber with fur made feathers stick to it, which was actually static electricity.

variants.question: True or false: the electron was discovered at the Cavendish Laboratory in 1897.

statement: Erwin Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935 and shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics for the Schrödinger equation.
quizQuestion: Who coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935. He shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac 'for the discovery of new productive forms of atomic theory', primarily for devising the Schrödinger equation. He was an Austrian-Irish theoretical physicist born on 12 August 1887.
wowFactor: The word 'entanglement' — now at the heart of quantum computing and teleportation research — was invented by the same physicist whose famous cat is both alive and dead.

variants.question: True or false: Schrödinger shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac.

statement: Wilhelm Röntgen discovered X-rays in 1895 and became the very first recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901.
quizQuestion: Who was the first ever recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1901 Röntgen became the first recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics 'in recognition of the extraordinary services he has rendered by the discovery of the remarkable rays subsequently named after him.' The element roentgenium is named in his honor.
wowFactor: X-rays were discovered in 1895, and within 6 years they had already won the first-ever Nobel Prize in Physics — the fastest any discovery went from lab bench to Nobel committee.

variants.question: True or false: the chemical element roentgenium is named after Wilhelm Röntgen.
The discovery of X-rays earned which physicist the inaugural Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901?
Röntgen's X-ray discovery took place in ___.
A Nobel committee selects the very first Physics laureate for discovering radiation that can see through skin. Who wins?

visualDescription: A pixel-art Victorian laboratory where Röntgen holds up an X-ray image of a skeletal hand, a glowing cathode tube illuminating the room, the Nobel medal glinting in the background.

statement: Cobalt is named after 'kobold' — the German word for goblin — because medieval miners believed goblins had poisoned the ore.
quizQuestion: Which element is named after a German word for 'goblin' because miners thought spirits had cursed their ore?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, miners called troublesome ores 'kobold ore' (German for goblin ore) because they were poor in metals and gave off poisonous arsenic fumes when smelted. In 1735, Georg Brandt found these ores contained a new metal, ultimately named cobalt after the kobold.
wowFactor: Medieval German miners blamed goblins for their poisonous ore — and scientists honored the superstition by permanently naming the element after the imaginary goblin.

variants.question: True or false: cobalt's name comes from the German word 'kobold', meaning goblin.

German miners in the 1700s find ore they think is cursed by goblins. Scientists name the new metal after the goblin. What metal?

visualDescription: A pixel-art medieval German mine tunnel with frightened miners backing away from a glowing blue ore seam, a mischievous goblin silhouette lurking in the shadows.

statement: Nickel is named after a mischievous sprite of German miner mythology because the ore looked like copper but yielded none — miners called it 'kupfernickel' (demon copper).
quizQuestion: Which metal is named after a mischievous sprite because miners were tricked by ore that looked like copper?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, nickel's name comes from a mischievous sprite of German miner mythology (similar to Old Nick). Nickel minerals can be green, like copper ores, and were known as 'kupfernickel' — Nickel's copper — because they produced no copper. Nickel was first isolated by Axel Fredrik Cronstedt in 1751.
wowFactor: Your 5-cent coin is named after a fictional demon — frustrated miners were so furious at the worthless green ore that fooled them they named it after an evil spirit, and the name stuck.

statement: China produced 54% of the world's steel in 2023, and more than 1.6 billion tons of steel are produced globally each year.
quizQuestion: What percentage of the world's steel did China produce in 2023?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, world steel production is currently centred in China, which produced 54% of the world's steel in 2023. More than 1.6 billion tons of steel are produced annually worldwide. Steel is an alloy of iron and carbon and is one of the most commonly manufactured materials in the world.
wowFactor: China produces more than half the world's steel — more than the entire rest of the planet combined — roughly a kilogram of new steel for every person on Earth every week.

statement: Francium is the second-rarest naturally occurring element; at any given moment, only about 1 ounce (28 g) of it exists in Earth's entire crust.
quizQuestion: How much francium exists naturally in Earth's entire crust at any one moment?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, francium is extremely rare — as little as 1 ounce (28 g) of it exists at any given time throughout Earth's crust. It exists only because francium-223 continually forms and decays in uranium ores. Its most stable isotope has a half-life of only 22 minutes.
wowFactor: The entire planet contains less than a shot-glass worth of francium at any moment — the most fleeting element in nature, constantly blinking in and out of existence.

variants.question: True or false: Francium's most stable isotope has a half-life of only 22 minutes.

visualDescription: A pixel-art periodic table with the francium square blinking like a firefly — barely there, perpetually decaying into other elements, a ghost of the periodic table.

statement: Francium was the last element to be discovered in nature rather than by synthesis — found in 1939 by Marguerite Perey in France.
quizQuestion: Francium holds the distinction of being the last element discovered in nature rather than how?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, francium was discovered by Marguerite Perey in France on January 7, 1939, and was the last element first discovered in nature, rather than by synthesis. All elements discovered after francium were produced artificially in nuclear reactions.
wowFactor: Francium closed a door on natural discovery — every element found after 1939 had to be made in a lab. Perey's 1939 discovery was the last time we ever found a new element in nature.

variants.question: True or false: Francium was the last element first found in nature, not synthesised.

statement: Radium was discovered by Marie and Pierre Curie in 1898 from ore mined at Jáchymov, and its most stable isotope has a half-life of 1,600 years.
quizQuestion: Who discovered radium in 1898 from uranium ore?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, radium was discovered by Marie and Pierre Curie in 1898 from ore mined at Jáchymov. They extracted the radium compound from uraninite and published the discovery at the French Academy of Sciences five days later. Radium-226, its most stable isotope, has a half-life of 1,600 years.
wowFactor: The radium Marie Curie isolated in 1898 is still only halfway through its radioactive decay — it takes 1,600 years for radium to halve, meaning her original samples are still quite radioactive today.

variants.question: True or false: Radium's most stable isotope, radium-226, has a half-life of 1,600 years.

statement: Cobalt was named after a German word for goblin because medieval miners blamed a mischievous sprite for the poisonous arsenic fumes from cobalt ores.
quizQuestion: Which element's name comes from a German word for goblin, blamed for poisoning miners?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the name cobalt derives from a type of ore considered a nuisance by 16th-century German silver miners — kobold ore, named after a spirit held responsible for the ore's behaviour. The ores were so named because they were poor in known metals and gave off poisonous arsenic-containing fumes when smelted.
wowFactor: Medieval miners named cobalt after a goblin because the ore kept killing them with arsenic fumes and they needed something to blame — the element's supernatural namesake stuck for 500 years.

statement: Nickel's name comes from 'Kupfernickel' — meaning 'Old Nick's copper' — because German miners cursed the ore for looking like copper but yielding none.

correctAnswer: It looked like copper but contained none
distractors: It had a devilish green glow
It was found only at night
acceptableAnswers: looked like copper without giving any
explanation: According to Wikipedia, nickel minerals can be green, like copper ores, and were known as Kupfernickel — 'Nickel's copper' — because they produced no copper. The element's name comes from a mischievous sprite of German miner mythology, Nickel (similar to Old Nick). Nickel was first isolated as an element in 1751 by Axel Fredrik Cronstedt.
wowFactor: Both cobalt and nickel are named after supernatural beings from German miner folklore — nature's most troll-like elements literally have troll names.

statement: Gallium melts in a human hand because its melting point is just 29.76°C — below normal body temperature of 37°C.
quizQuestion: Why does gallium metal melt when held in a human hand?
correctAnswer: Its melting point is below body temperature
distractors: Body heat activates a chemical reaction
It is a liquid at room temperature always
It reacts with oxygen in air
explanation: According to Wikipedia, gallium's melting point is 29.7646°C — below normal human body temperature of 37°C. This means elemental gallium will literally melt in a person's hands at normal body temperature, transforming from a solid silvery metal to a liquid.
wowFactor: Gallium is a solid metal that melts in your hand — not because of a chemical reaction but simply because your body is warmer than its melting point of 29.76°C.

statement: Gallium was discovered in 1875 by French chemist Paul-Émile Lecoq de Boisbaudran in Paris, and named after Gaul — the Latin name for France.
quizQuestion: Which country's Latin name inspired the name of the element gallium?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, gallium was discovered by the French chemist Paul-Émile Lecoq de Boisbaudran in Paris, France in 1875, and was named after Gaul, the Latin name for France. It was predicted to exist by Mendeleev before its discovery and confirmed his periodic table model.
wowFactor: Gallium was predicted to exist before anyone found it — Mendeleev left a gap for it in his 1869 periodic table and described its properties in advance. When it was discovered in 1875, it matched his predictions almost exactly.

statement: The electron was discovered in 1897 by J. J. Thomson at the Cavendish Laboratory — it was the first subatomic particle ever identified.
quizQuestion: Who discovered the electron in 1897, identifying the first subatomic particle?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the electron was discovered in 1897 by J. J. Thomson at the Cavendish Laboratory of Experimental Physics. The electron is an elementary particle with a negative electric charge of -1 elementary charge and a mass approximately 1/1836 that of a proton.
wowFactor: J. J. Thomson's 1897 discovery of the electron shattered the 'atom is indivisible' assumption — the moment physics realised atoms had parts, everything changed.

statement: Arsenic ranked number 1 on the US Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry's 2001 list of most hazardous substances at Superfund sites.
quizQuestion: Which element ranked #1 on the US list of most hazardous substances at contaminated sites in 2001?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the United States Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry ranked arsenic number 1 in its 2001 prioritized list of hazardous substances at Superfund sites. Arsenic is classified as a group-A carcinogen and its contamination of groundwater affects millions of people worldwide.
wowFactor: Arsenic — the legendary poison of mystery novels — is officially ranked the most hazardous substance at US contaminated sites, and naturally contaminates the drinking water of millions of people today.

variants.question: True or false: Arsenic is classified as a group-A carcinogen by the US EPA.

statement: From 1960 to 1983, the official definition of the metre was based on a specific wavelength of krypton-86 light.
quizQuestion: From 1960 to 1983, the metre was officially defined using the light of which element?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, from 1960 to 1983, the official definition of the metre was based on the wavelength of one spectral line of krypton-86. Krypton was chosen because of the high power and relative ease of operation of krypton discharge tubes. In 1983 the definition was changed to be based on the speed of light.
wowFactor: For 23 years, every ruler and tape measure in the world was secretly defined by the orange-red glow of krypton gas — not Superman's home planet, but a real noble gas.

variants.question: True or false: From 1960 to 1983, the metre was officially defined using a wavelength of krypton-86 light.
Krypton's name comes from Ancient Greek meaning 'the ___ one.'

statement: Bromine is one of only two elements that are liquid at standard temperature and pressure — the other is mercury.
quizQuestion: Which two elements are liquid at standard temperature and pressure?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, at standard conditions for temperature and pressure bromine is a liquid; the only other element that is liquid under these conditions is mercury. Bromine is a volatile red-brown liquid that evaporates readily to form a similarly coloured vapour with a sharp, pungent smell.
wowFactor: Out of 118 known elements, only two are liquid at room temperature: mercury (a metal) and bromine (a non-metal). They share this unusual distinction with nothing else on the periodic table.

variants.question: True or false: Bromine and mercury are the only elements that are liquid at standard temperature and pressure.
Bromine's name derives from Ancient Greek meaning '___, referring to its sharp smell.

Which halogen element, atomic number 35, shares with mercury the distinction of being liquid at standard conditions?

statement: Vanadium was named after Vanadís — the Norse goddess of beauty and fertility (another name for Freyja) — because of its wide range of brilliant colours.
quizQuestion: Which element is named after a Norse goddess of beauty, due to its many vivid colours?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, vanadium was named 'vanadium' after Vanadís (Freyja), the Scandinavian goddess of beauty and fertility. The name was chosen by Nils Gabriel Sefström in 1830 because of the wide range of beautiful colours found in vanadium compounds. The element was originally discovered by Andrés Manuel del Río in 1801 but he was convinced it was just chromium.
wowFactor: Vanadium was discovered twice — first in 1801, but the discoverer was talked out of his own discovery. When rediscovered 30 years later, it was named after the goddess of beauty for its rainbow of compound colours.

variants.question: True or false: Vanadium is named after a Norse goddess because of the many colours its compounds display.
Vanadium is named after Vanadís, another name for the Norse goddess ___.

distractors: The town where it was found

variants.question: Which transition metal, atomic number 23, is named after the Norse fertility goddess Freyja?

statement: Tungsten has the highest melting point of any known element, at 3,422°C — hot enough to survive inside a running jet engine.
quizQuestion: Which element has the highest melting point of all known elements?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, tungsten's melting point of 3,422°C (6,192°F) is the highest of all known elements. It also has the highest boiling point at 5,930°C. Its density is 19.254 g/cm³, comparable to uranium. Despite its symbol W, tungsten's alternative name 'wolfram' comes from the ore wolframite.
wowFactor: Tungsten melts at 3,422°C — so hot that the Sun's surface would feel comparably warm. That's why it's used in lightbulb filaments and rocket nozzles.

variants.question: True or false: Tungsten has the highest melting point of any element on the periodic table.
Tungsten's melting point is {3,422} degrees Celsius, the highest of any element.

What does tungsten's chemical symbol W stand for?

statement: Tungsten's chemical symbol is W because it was historically called 'wolfram,' derived from the ore wolframite.
quizQuestion: Why does tungsten have the chemical symbol W?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, tungsten is 'also called wolfram,' and its symbol W comes from German: Wolfram, derived from the ore wolframite. The element was identified as distinct in 1781 and first isolated as a metal in 1783, with atomic number 74.
wowFactor: Every time someone writes 'W' on a chemistry test, they're using a German word for a rock — tungsten spent centuries being called 'wolfram' before getting its English name.

variants.question: True or false: Tungsten's symbol W comes from its historical German name 'wolfram.'
Which element is also known as wolfram, giving it the chemical symbol W?
In what year was tungsten first isolated as a metal?
Tungsten's atomic number is {74}.

visualDescription: A periodic table tile showing 'W' with a magnifying glass revealing the word 'Wolfram' beneath — chemistry's most misleading symbol.

statement: Tungsten also has the highest boiling point of any element, at 5,930°C — well above the surface temperature of the Sun.
quizQuestion: Tungsten's boiling point of 5,930°C is higher than the surface temperature of what?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, tungsten has 'the highest boiling point, at 5,930°C (10,706°F; 6,203 K).' The Sun's surface temperature is approximately 5,500°C — meaning tungsten would remain liquid on the solar surface before finally boiling away.
wowFactor: Tungsten's boiling point is higher than the surface of the Sun — you could dip it in the solar surface and it would not evaporate.

variants.question: True or false: Tungsten's boiling point of 5,930°C exceeds the surface temperature of the Sun.
Which element has the highest boiling point of any known element, at 5,930°C?
Tungsten's boiling point is {5,930} degrees Celsius.
What is tungsten's density in grams per cubic centimetre, comparable to uranium?

visualDescription: A pixel-art Sun blazing in space, a tiny tungsten wire still glowing red-hot beside it — the only element that survives the solar surface.

statement: Radium was discovered in 1898 by Marie and Pierre Curie, and named after radioactivity — the phenomenon they were studying.
quizQuestion: Which husband-and-wife team discovered radium in 1898?
explanation: According to Wikidata, radium's discoverers were Pierre Curie and Marie Curie, in 1898. According to Wikipedia, radium (atomic number 88) is named after 'radioactivity.' Its most stable isotope, radium-226, has a half-life of 1,600 years. It was widely used in self-luminous paints until its dangers became understood.
wowFactor: The Curies named radium after the very concept of radioactivity — a word Marie Curie herself coined. She literally named both the element and the field of study.

variants.question: True or false: Radium was discovered by Marie and Pierre Curie in 1898.
Marie and Pierre Curie's 1898 discovery was which radioactive element?
What is the half-life of radium-226, radium's most stable isotope?
Radium has atomic number {88}.

visualDescription: Marie Curie in a pixel-art laboratory, a glowing green vial in her hand — the night she discovered radium, the lab faintly lit by the element itself.

statement: Radium was widely used in self-luminous paints for watch dials after its discovery — before its lethal radioactivity became understood.
quizQuestion: What was radium famously used in before its radioactive dangers were recognized?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, radium 'was widely used in self-luminous paints following its discovery,' particularly for clock and watch dials. Its ionizing radiation excites fluorescent chemicals, causing them to glow. Factory workers who painted dials — the 'Radium Girls' — suffered severe radiation injuries before the risks were understood.
wowFactor: Factory workers used to lick their radium paintbrushes to get a finer point — they had no idea the glowing paint was slowly killing them.

variants.question: True or false: Radium was used in self-luminous watch dial paint before its dangers were known.
Which radioactive element was used in luminous watch paints, harming factory workers known as the Radium Girls?
What property of radium makes it excite fluorescent chemicals to glow?
All isotopes of radium are radioactive, with radium-{226} being the most stable.

sensitivityNote: References historical industrial poisoning; factual and widely documented.
visualDescription: Pixel-art women in a 1920s factory painting watch dials with glowing green radium paint, unaware of the danger — history's most lethal workplace craft.

statement: Bromine is one of only two elements that is a liquid at room temperature, appearing as a volatile red-brown liquid.
quizQuestion: Bromine shares the distinction of being liquid at room temperature with which other element?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, bromine is 'a volatile red-brown liquid at room temperature that evaporates readily to form a similarly coloured vapour.' Bromine and mercury are the only two elements that are liquid at standard conditions. Bromine's name comes from Ancient Greek βρῶμος (bromos) meaning 'stench.'
wowFactor: Of 118 elements on the periodic table, only two are liquid at room temperature — and one is a stinky red-brown halogen whose name literally means 'stench.'

variants.question: True or false: Bromine is one of only two elements that is liquid at room temperature.
Which red-brown halogen element is liquid at room temperature, alongside mercury?
What does bromine's name mean in Ancient Greek?
Bromine has atomic number {35}.

visualDescription: A pixel-art lab flask of swirling red-brown liquid with purple vapors rising — bromine, one of only two elements that refuses to be solid at room temperature.

statement: Bromine's name comes from Ancient Greek for 'stench,' due to its intensely sharp and pungent smell.
quizQuestion: Bromine's name derives from an Ancient Greek word meaning what?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, bromine's 'name was derived from Ancient Greek βρῶμος (bromos) meaning stench, referring to its sharp and pungent smell.' It was independently isolated by Carl Jacob Löwig in 1825 and Antoine Jérôme Balard in 1826.
wowFactor: Chemists were so repelled by bromine's smell that they named it 'stench' in Ancient Greek — it may be the only element named purely for how bad it reeks.

variants.question: True or false: Bromine's name comes from the Ancient Greek word for 'stench.'
Which halogen element has a name literally meaning 'stench' in Ancient Greek?
In which years was bromine independently discovered by two different chemists?
Bromine is a member of the {halogens} group of elements.

visualDescription: A pixel-art chemist recoiling from a flask of swirling red vapors, nose pinched — the ancient Greeks would have agreed: it really does stench.

statement: From 1960 to 1983, the official definition of the metre was based on the wavelength of krypton-86 light.
quizQuestion: Which noble gas was used to define the official length of one metre from 1960 to 1983?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, 'from 1960 to 1983, the official definition of the metre was based on' krypton. The metre was defined as 1,650,763.73 wavelengths of orange-red light emitted by krypton-86. This was replaced by the speed-of-light definition in 1983.
wowFactor: The metre — the world's most fundamental unit of length — was once defined by the glow of krypton gas. Superman's home planet is also the name of a real noble gas that once defined reality.

variants.question: True or false: From 1960 to 1983, the metre was officially defined using krypton-86 light.
Which element's light wavelength defined the official metre between 1960 and 1983?
What physical constant replaced krypton in defining the metre after 1983?
Krypton has atomic number {36}.

visualDescription: A pixel-art lab with a glowing orange-red krypton discharge tube, a ruler beside it — the world once measured all length by this gas's glow.

statement: Krypton's name comes from Ancient Greek meaning 'the hidden one,' because it was extremely difficult to find in nature.
quizQuestion: Krypton's name comes from Ancient Greek meaning what?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, krypton derives from 'Ancient Greek: κρυπτός, romanized: kryptos meaning the hidden one.' It was discovered in 1898 by William Ramsay and Morris Travers. It is a colorless, odorless noble gas that occurs only in trace amounts in the atmosphere.
wowFactor: Krypton was named 'the hidden one' because it hides so well in the air — even Superman didn't know his home planet shared a name with a real secret element.

variants.question: True or false: Krypton's name comes from Ancient Greek meaning 'the hidden one.'
Which noble gas has a name meaning 'the hidden one' in Ancient Greek?
In what year was krypton discovered by William Ramsay?
Krypton is a colorless, {odorless} noble gas that occurs in trace amounts in the atmosphere.

visualDescription: A pixel-art noble gas atom glowing faintly in darkness, nearly invisible — krypton, the element that hides in plain air.

statement: Erwin Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935, the same year he also described his famous cat thought experiment.
quizQuestion: Which physicist coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Schrödinger 'coined the term quantum entanglement in 1935.' He is also recognized for devising the Schrödinger equation. He shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac 'for the discovery of new productive forms of atomic theory.'
wowFactor: Schrödinger gave us both the most famous cat in physics and the term 'quantum entanglement' in the same year — 1935 was a productive twelve months.

variants.question: True or false: Erwin Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935.
The term 'quantum entanglement' was first coined in 1935 by which physicist?
Which physicist shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Erwin Schrödinger?
Schrödinger shared the {1933} Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac.

visualDescription: A pixel-art physicist writes on a chalkboard — one side shows a cat in a box, the other the word 'Entanglement' — 1935's most productive mind at work.

statement: Erwin Schrödinger was born in Vienna in 1887 and died in Vienna in 1961 — his life came full circle to the city of his birth.
quizQuestion: In which city was Erwin Schrödinger both born and died?
explanation: According to Wikidata, Schrödinger's place of birth and place of death were both Vienna. He was born on 12 August 1887 and died on 4 January 1961. Despite spending decades abroad in Berlin, Oxford, and Dublin, he returned to Austria and died in his birth city.
wowFactor: Schrödinger fled Nazi Germany, spent years in Ireland, and ultimately came home to die in the same Vienna streets where he was born 73 years earlier.

variants.question: True or false: Erwin Schrödinger was both born and died in Vienna.
Which quantum physicist was born on 12 August 1887 and died in the same city as his birth?
At which university did Schrödinger study, according to Wikidata?
Schrödinger was born on {12} August 1887 in Vienna.

visualDescription: A pixel-art Vienna cityscape split — a young boy on the left in 1887, an elderly professor on the right in 1961 — the same city bookending a remarkable life.

statement: Platinum's name comes from the Spanish word 'platina,' meaning 'little silver' — because Spanish colonists thought it was just impure silver.
quizQuestion: Platinum's name comes from a Spanish word meaning what?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, platinum's 'name originates from Spanish platina, a diminutive of plata meaning silver.' Spanish colonists initially dismissed it as inferior silver. Platinum has atomic number 78, a mass of 195.084 daltons, and is one of the rarest elements in Earth's crust at about 5 μg/kg average abundance.
wowFactor: Spanish conquistadors found platinum in South American rivers and called it 'little silver' — a dismissive nickname for what is now more valuable than gold.

variants.question: True or false: Platinum's name comes from the Spanish word 'platina,' meaning 'little silver.'
Which precious metal was named 'little silver' by Spanish colonists who thought it was inferior?
What is platinum's average abundance in Earth's crust?
Platinum has atomic number {78}.

visualDescription: A pixel-art Spanish conquistador tosses aside a handful of silvery metal — platinum, dismissed as junk, now worth more than gold.

statement: Bismuth is the most diamagnetic element and one of the least thermally conductive metals, forming vivid iridescent crystals when oxidized under heat.
quizQuestion: Which element is the most diamagnetic of all elements and forms vivid iridescent crystals?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, bismuth is 'both the most diamagnetic element and one of the least thermally conductive' metals. Surface oxidation under heat gives bismuth 'a vividly iridescent appearance due to thin-film interference.' It has atomic number 83 and is 86% as dense as lead.
wowFactor: Bismuth is the most magnetically repelled element on the periodic table — and when you heat it, it turns into a rainbow-colored crystal that looks like something from a sci-fi movie.

variants.question: True or false: Bismuth is the most diamagnetic element on the periodic table.
Which post-transition metal, with atomic number 83, is the most diamagnetic element?
What optical phenomenon makes heated bismuth appear iridescent?
Bismuth is {86}% as dense as lead.

visualDescription: A pixel-art bismuth crystal gleams with rainbow layers of gold, blue, and purple — the most diamagnetic metal forming one of nature's most beautiful structures.

statement: Erwin Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935, the same year he devised the famous Schrödinger's cat thought experiment.
quizQuestion: Who coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935 — the same year he devised the Schrödinger's cat thought experiment. He also shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac for 'the discovery of new productive forms of atomic theory.'
wowFactor: The same man who invented the 'Schrödinger's cat' paradox also coined the term 'quantum entanglement' — both in the same year, 1935!

variants.question: True or false: Erwin Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935.
Which famous thought experiment did Schrödinger devise in 1935 alongside coining quantum entanglement?
Schrödinger won the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics sharing it with which other physicist?
Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum ___' in 1935.

visualDescription: Pixel-art Schrödinger at a chalkboard in 1935 — a cat in a box on one side, the words 'quantum entanglement' freshly chalked on the other, both equations appearing in the same moment.

statement: Since 1967, the international definition of one second has been based on the vibrations of caesium-133 atoms — about 9.2 billion vibrations per second.
quizQuestion: Which element's atomic vibrations define the international standard second since 1967?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1967 the International System of Units began using a specific hyperfine transition of neutral caesium-133 atoms to define the basic unit of time, the second. Caesium atomic clocks are among the most accurate timekeeping devices ever built.
wowFactor: Every clock, GPS satellite, and smartphone in the world tracks time by counting caesium atom vibrations — 9.2 billion per second, a standard set in 1967!

variants.question: True or false: Since 1967, the second has been defined using vibrations of caesium-133 atoms.
What type of technology uses caesium's atomic vibrations to keep extremely precise time?
A physicist in 1967 redefines what the word 'second' means. Which element's atoms set the standard?
Caesium has the ___ electronegativity of all stable elements, at 0.79 on the Pauling scale.

visualDescription: Pixel-art atomic clock with a glowing caesium vapor tube at its core — each vibration ticking off billionths of a second, the most precise timekeeper in history.

statement: Caesium is one of only five elemental metals that are liquid at or near room temperature, melting at just 28.5°C.
quizQuestion: At what temperature does caesium melt, making it one of just five metals liquid near room temperature?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, caesium has a melting point of 28.5°C (83.3°F), which makes it one of only five elemental metals that are liquid at or near room temperature. It is the least electronegative stable element, with a value of 0.79 on the Pauling scale.
wowFactor: Caesium melts at 28.5°C — meaning this metal would turn to liquid on a hot summer day! Only four other metals are as easy to melt.

variants.question: True or false: Caesium can melt at room temperature on a hot day.
Which alkali metal melts at just 28.5°C, making it liquid on warm days?
A chemist stores a soft gold-silver metal that turns liquid at 28.5°C. Which element is it?
Caesium has the ___ electronegativity of all stable chemical elements.

visualDescription: Pixel-art laboratory beaker holding a shimmering golden liquid metal on a summer afternoon — caesium, one of only five metals liquid at room temperature.

statement: Radon is a radioactive noble gas produced naturally by the decay of uranium in rocks and soil, and is the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking.
quizQuestion: Which radioactive noble gas produced by uranium decay is a leading cause of lung cancer?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, radon (symbol Rn, atomic number 86) is a radioactive noble gas produced by the decay of uranium-238. It is colorless and odorless, and epidemiological studies have shown a clear association between breathing high concentrations of radon and lung cancer. It is classified as an environmental carcinogen.
wowFactor: Radon is a colorless, odorless gas silently seeping up through the floors of millions of homes right now — and it's the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking!

variants.question: True or false: Radon is a colorless, odorless radioactive gas produced by uranium decay.
What is the atomic number of radon?
Radon is the fifth radioactive element ever discovered. Who discovered it in 1899?
Radon is named after ___, from which it is produced through radioactive decay.

visualDescription: Pixel-art cross-section of a house showing invisible radon gas seeping up through the basement floor from granite bedrock below — a silent, invisible health hazard.

statement: The US Agency for Toxic Substances ranked arsenic #1 on its 2001 list of the most hazardous substances at Superfund contamination sites.
quizQuestion: Which element ranked #1 on the US government's 2001 list of most hazardous Superfund substances?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the United States Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry ranked arsenic number 1 in its 2001 prioritized list of hazardous substances at Superfund sites. Arsenic is also classified as a Group-A carcinogen and contaminates groundwater affecting millions worldwide.
wowFactor: Out of every toxic substance in the US — lead, mercury, benzene, all of them — arsenic ranked #1 most hazardous in 2001. The classic 'poison' is also the deadliest environmental contaminant!

variants.question: True or false: Arsenic was ranked the #1 most hazardous substance at US Superfund sites in 2001.
What is the atomic number of arsenic?
Arsenic was known since ancient times to be poisonous. Who is credited with its discovery?
Arsenic has the chemical symbol ___ and atomic number 33.

visualDescription: Pixel-art periodic table with arsenic glowing red in position #33 — a skull-and-crossbones beside it, number-1 ranking stamped on the toxic chart.

statement: The name 'arsenic' derives from Arabic and Persian words meaning 'gold' and 'virility,' reflecting ancient uses of the golden-colored ore orpiment.
quizQuestion: What ancient concepts does the word 'arsenic' derive its name from?
explanation: According to Wikidata, arsenic was named after orpiment (a golden-yellow mineral) and 'virility.' The word traces back to the Greek arsenikon, itself from Arabic and Persian words for 'golden.' The element's symbol As and atomic number are 33.
wowFactor: The word 'arsenic' literally comes from words meaning 'gold' and 'virility' — ancient people actually believed this deadly poison had health-boosting powers!

variants.question: True or false: The name 'arsenic' derives partly from a word meaning 'virility.'
The golden-yellow ore orpiment gave arsenic part of its name. What does 'orpiment' mean in Latin?
Ancient physicians believed arsenic had health benefits and named it partly for virility. Why does this seem ironic?
variants.correctAnswer: Arsenic is a deadly poison and carcinogen
distractors: Arsenic is safe in small doses

variants.question: Arsenic was first described by ___ in around 1300 AD, making it a known element since the medieval period.

visualDescription: Pixel-art medieval alchemist admiring a golden-yellow lump of orpiment ore — the beautiful mineral that gave its name to the most famous poison in history.

statement: An unusual abundance of iridium in the clay layer at the Cretaceous-Paleogene boundary gave rise to the theory that a massive asteroid impact caused the dinosaurs' extinction 66 million years ago.
quizQuestion: Which rare element's unusual abundance at the K-Pg boundary suggested an asteroid killed the dinosaurs?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, iridium is found in meteorites in much higher abundance than in Earth's crust. The unusually high abundance of iridium in the clay layer at the Cretaceous-Paleogene boundary gave rise to the Alvarez hypothesis — that the impact of a massive extraterrestrial object caused the extinction of non-avian dinosaurs 66 million years ago.
wowFactor: The whole 'asteroid killed the dinosaurs' theory began with one observation: a thin layer of rock worldwide contains way too much iridium to be from Earth — it had to come from space!

variants.question: True or false: An iridium-rich layer in rock strata helped confirm that an asteroid killed the dinosaurs.
The Alvarez hypothesis about dinosaur extinction was triggered by abnormal amounts of which element?
A geologist finds way too much of a rare element in a 66-million-year-old rock layer. Which element?
Iridium is named after ___, the Greek goddess of the rainbow, because of its colorful compounds.

visualDescription: Pixel-art cross-section of ancient rock strata — a paper-thin glowing silver layer of iridium separating two eras, a giant fireball implied in the sky above.

statement: Iridium is one of the most corrosion-resistant metals known, maintaining resistance even at temperatures up to 2,000°C, and produces only about 6,800 kg per year worldwide.
quizQuestion: Approximately how many kilograms of iridium are produced annually worldwide?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, iridium is one of the rarest elements in Earth's crust, with an estimated annual production of only 6,800 kilograms in 2023. It is also one of the most corrosion-resistant metals, even at temperatures as high as 2,000°C.
wowFactor: The entire world produces only 6,800 kg of iridium per year — less than the weight of a single large truck — yet this tiny supply holds the clue to what killed the dinosaurs!

variants.question: True or false: Iridium is one of the most corrosion-resistant metals, even at 2,000°C.
What is iridium's atomic number?
Which dense, brittle metal of the platinum group is considered the second-densest naturally occurring metal?
Iridium's name comes from the Greek word for ___, due to the variety of colors in its compounds.

visualDescription: Pixel-art scales of justice weighing 6,800 kg of silvery iridium against all the other metals on Earth — the rarest chunk of metal glowing faintly on one side.

statement: Ernest Rutherford named the proton in 1920; the word 'proton' comes from the Greek for 'first.'
quizQuestion: What does the word 'proton' mean in Greek?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the word proton is Greek for 'first,' and the name was given to the hydrogen nucleus by Ernest Rutherford in 1920. Rutherford had previously discovered that hydrogen nuclei could be extracted from nitrogen nuclei by atomic collisions, making protons candidates for being fundamental building blocks of all atoms.
wowFactor: The proton got its name from Greek for 'first' because Rutherford thought it was the fundamental building block of all matter — the first particle of everything!

variants.question: True or false: The word 'proton' comes from the Greek word meaning 'first.'
Who named the proton in 1920, deriving the word from Greek for 'first'?
Rutherford names a new particle in 1920, choosing a Greek word meaning 'first.' What is this particle?
A proton is made of two ___ quarks and one down quark.

visualDescription: Pixel-art Rutherford in his Manchester lab watching a hydrogen nucleus fly out of nitrogen during a collision experiment — the world's first named proton caught in the act.

statement: A proton is approximately 1,836 times heavier than an electron, despite both carrying the same magnitude of electric charge.
quizQuestion: Approximately how many times heavier is a proton than an electron?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the proton's mass is approximately 1,836 times the mass of an electron (the proton-to-electron mass ratio). Despite this enormous mass difference, both particles carry exactly the same magnitude of electric charge — the proton positive and the electron negative.
wowFactor: A proton is 1,836 times heavier than an electron — yet they carry exactly equal and opposite electric charges. Size and charge have nothing to do with each other in the quantum world!

variants.question: True or false: A proton is approximately 1,836 times heavier than an electron.
What is the electric charge of a proton in elementary charge units?
A proton is made up of three quarks: two up quarks and one of which other type?
Protons and neutrons together are called ___, as they are found in atomic nuclei.

visualDescription: Pixel-art scale comparison: a tiny electron next to a massive proton 1,836 times its size, both glowing with equal positive and negative charge signs.

statement: Erwin Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935, the same year he devised his famous cat thought experiment.
quizQuestion: Which physicist coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935 — the same year he published his famous Schrödinger's Cat thought experiment. He shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac.
wowFactor: Schrödinger gave us both 'quantum entanglement' and the famous dead-and-alive cat paradox in the same year — 1935 was his most mind-bending year ever!

variants.question: What Nobel Prize did Schrödinger share with Paul Dirac in 1933?
True or false: Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935.
Who coined 'quantum entanglement' and devised the famous cat thought experiment?
A 1935 physics paper introduces both a cat paradox and a new term for spooky particle connections. Who wrote it?

visualDescription: A physicist in a white coat sketches a cat inside a sealed box on a chalkboard, quantum wave equations swirling around it, two entangled particles glowing in the corner of the diagram.

statement: Wilhelm Röntgen was the first person ever to receive the Nobel Prize in Physics, awarded in 1901 for his discovery of X-rays.
quizQuestion: Who was the first ever recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Röntgen received the first Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901 for his 1895 discovery of X-rays. The element roentgenium (Rg) is named in his honor.
wowFactor: Röntgen didn't just discover X-rays — he won Nobel Prize in Physics number 1, and a chemical element is named after him!

variants.question: In what year did Röntgen receive the first Nobel Prize in Physics?
True or false: Wilhelm Röntgen was the first-ever recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics.
Which discovery earned the very first Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901?
The chemical element roentgenium (Rg) is named after Wilhelm ___, discoverer of X-rays.

visualDescription: A Victorian-era physicist examines an X-ray image of a human hand in a dark laboratory, the ghostly bones glowing white on the photographic plate while unknown rays radiate from a glass tube behind him.

statement: Radon is a colorless, odorless radioactive noble gas that seeps from uranium-bearing soil into buildings and is the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking.
quizQuestion: Which radioactive gas, seeping from the ground into buildings, is the second leading cause of lung cancer?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, radon (atomic number 86) is a colorless, odorless radioactive noble gas produced by the decay of uranium in soil. Epidemiological studies show a clear association between high radon concentrations in buildings and lung cancer.
wowFactor: Your home might be slowly filling with invisible radioactive gas right now — radon seeps up from uranium in the ground through your floors and is the second biggest cause of lung cancer worldwide!

variants.question: What is radon's atomic number?
True or false: Radon is a colorless and odorless radioactive gas that can accumulate in buildings.
Which element, named after radium, is a radioactive noble gas and a leading indoor air carcinogen?
Radon is produced by the radioactive decay of ___ in soil and rock.

visualDescription: Invisible gas marked by glowing red particles seeps up through a basement floor from rocky soil below, a radon detector on the wall flashing red while the homeowner sleeps upstairs unaware.

statement: Vanadium was named after Vanadís, a Norse goddess (Freyja), because of the wide range of vivid colors found in its chemical compounds.
quizQuestion: Which element is named after a Norse goddess because of its colorful compounds?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, vanadium was named after Vanadís (an Old Norse name for Freyja) in 1830 by Nils Gabriel Sefström, reflecting the element's beautiful range of colors in its compounds. The element symbol is V.
wowFactor: Vanadium is literally named after the Norse goddess of beauty because its compounds show every color of the rainbow — chemists couldn't pick just one hue, so they named it after the goddess herself!

variants.question: What is vanadium's atomic number?
True or false: Vanadium was named after the Norse goddess Freyja (Vanadís) because of its compounds' many colors.
Which element was named after the Norse goddess Vanadís because of its colorful chemistry?
Vanadium's name comes from Vanadís, an Old Norse name for ___, the goddess of beauty.

visualDescription: A series of test tubes glow in vivid blues, greens, purples, yellows, and oranges — all vanadium compounds — arranged like a rainbow in a chemistry lab, with a Norse goddess illustration pinned to the wall above.

statement: Plutonium was named after Pluto — then considered a planet — following the pattern of naming actinides after planets beyond uranium and neptunium.
quizQuestion: Why was plutonium named after Pluto when it was discovered in 1941?
correctAnswer: Pluto was the next planet beyond Neptune
distractors: Pluto was newly discovered that year
Pluto is the Roman god of the underworld
It was discovered near a plutonium mine
Pluto was the coldest known body
It was related to planetary physics
It was found in a meteorite
explanation: According to Wikipedia, plutonium (atomic number 94) was first synthesized in 1941 at UC Berkeley. Since uranium was named for Uranus and neptunium for Neptune, element 94 was named after Pluto — the next planet out — following the same pattern.
wowFactor: Plutonium owes its name to a once-planet: uranium → Uranus, neptunium → Neptune, plutonium → Pluto. When Pluto was demoted in 2006, plutonium kept its name anyway!

variants.question: What is plutonium's atomic number?
True or false: Plutonium was named after Pluto because uranium was named after Uranus and neptunium after Neptune.
Which radioactive element follows the same planetary naming pattern as uranium and neptunium?
Plutonium follows the naming pattern: uranium → Uranus, neptunium → Neptune, plutonium → ___.

visualDescription: Three periodic table squares glow in sequence — Uranium (U), Neptunium (Np), Plutonium (Pu) — with the outer solar system's planets Uranus, Neptune, and Pluto floating behind each one.

statement: Polonium was discovered in 1898 by Marie and Pierre Curie and named after Marie's homeland Poland, which at the time was not an independent country.
quizQuestion: Why did Marie Curie name the element polonium after Poland?
correctAnswer: Poland was not independent and she wanted to highlight it
distractors: It was found in Polish mineral deposits
She was born in Polonium province
Poland had the largest uranium mines
Her PhD thesis was about Poland
acceptableAnswers: to draw attention to poland's situation
explanation: According to Wikipedia, polonium was discovered on 18 July 1898 and named after Marie Curie's homeland Poland — then partitioned between Russia, Prussia, and Austria with no independent existence. It was the first element discovered solely by its radioactivity.
wowFactor: Marie Curie named polonium after her occupied homeland as a political statement — Poland didn't exist as a country at the time, and she used a Nobel-Prize-winning element to put it on the world map!

variants.question: In what year did Marie and Pierre Curie discover polonium?
True or false: Polonium was named after Poland, which was not an independent country when it was discovered.
Which element did Marie Curie name after her occupied homeland?
Polonium was the first element discovered solely by its ___, not by chemical or physical means.

visualDescription: Marie Curie writes 'polonium' on a laboratory blackboard in Paris 1898, a map of partitioned Poland behind her showing it divided between three empires, her homeland erased from maps but immortalised in the periodic table.

statement: Manganese is a key component of the oxygen-evolving complex in plants — the molecular cluster responsible for producing Earth's atmospheric oxygen via photosynthesis.

explanation: According to Wikipedia, manganese is used by plants in the oxygen-evolving complex (OEC) — a Mn-O cluster that splits water molecules during photosynthesis, releasing the oxygen we breathe.
wowFactor: Almost every breath you take was enabled by manganese — the metal at the core of the plant molecule that splits water and releases oxygen into the atmosphere!

statement: Mendeleev predicted the existence and properties of germanium 17 years before it was discovered, calling it 'ekasilicon.'

explanation: According to Wikipedia, Mendeleev predicted germanium's existence in 1869 from its position on the periodic table and named it ekasilicon. Clemens Winkler discovered it in 1886 in the mineral argyrodite, confirming Mendeleev's predictions with remarkable accuracy.
wowFactor: Mendeleev's periodic table was so powerful he predicted an element 17 years before anyone found it — and when germanium was discovered, its properties matched his prediction almost exactly!

statement: Bismuth was once considered the heaviest stable element, but in 2003 it was found to be very slightly radioactive with a half-life roughly a billion times longer than the age of the universe.
quizQuestion: What was discovered about bismuth in 2003 that overturned decades of scientific consensus?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, bismuth (atomic number 83) was long believed to have the highest atomic mass of any stable element. In 2003, scientists found that bismuth-209 is very slightly radioactive with an alpha-decay half-life roughly a billion times longer than the estimated age of the universe.
wowFactor: Scientists thought bismuth was completely stable for over a century — then in 2003 they proved it's actually radioactive, but so slowly it would take a billion times the age of the universe to fully decay!

statement: Xenon, a noble gas, is used both as a general anesthetic in surgery and as a propellant for ion thrusters in spacecraft.
quizQuestion: Which noble gas is used both as a surgical anesthetic and as spacecraft propellant?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, xenon (atomic number 54) is used as a general anesthetic because of its narcotic properties, and as an ion thruster propellant in spacecraft because it is easily ionised and has high atomic mass. It was discovered in 1898 by Ramsay and Travers.
wowFactor: The same inert gas that can put you to sleep in an operating room is also used to propel spacecraft through the solar system — xenon works equally well in a hospital and in deep space!

statement: About 28% of natural rubidium is the slightly radioactive isotope Rb-87, with a half-life of 48.8 billion years — more than three times the age of the universe.
quizQuestion: How does rubidium-87's half-life compare to the age of the universe?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, rubidium-87 has a half-life of 48.8 billion years. Since the universe is approximately 13.8 billion years old, this is more than three times the estimated age of the universe.
wowFactor: 28% of all rubidium on Earth is slowly decaying — but so slowly that it takes more than three entire lifetimes of the universe for half of it to change!

statement: Strontium-90, the radioactive form of strontium, is one of the most dangerous components of nuclear fallout because the body absorbs it like calcium.

It attacks the lungs like asbestos
It is chemically similar to sodium
explanation: According to Wikipedia, strontium-90 is radioactive and one of the most dangerous components of nuclear fallout because strontium is absorbed by the body in a similar manner to calcium — it concentrates in bones and can cause bone cancer and leukemia.
wowFactor: Your bones can't tell strontium-90 apart from calcium — so after a nuclear event, your skeleton could be absorbing radioactive atoms it thinks are just nutrition!
