wowFactor: Earth is 4.54 billion years old — a number derived from crystals in Australian rocks and meteorites. If you compressed Earth's entire history into a single year, humans appear in the last 23 minutes.

variants.question: True or false: Earth's age of 4.54 billion years was determined through radiometric dating.
The oldest surviving Earth material — ancient zircon crystals — is found in ___.
If Earth's 4.54-billion-year history were compressed into one calendar year, when would humans appear?
How old is Earth, established through radiometric dating of ancient zircons and meteorites?

visualDescription: A tiny zircon crystal under a microscope in an Australian laboratory — the oldest Earth material at 4.4 billion years old, a geological witness to the formation of a planet.

statement: A star's mass determines its fate: stars like our Sun become white dwarfs, massive stars explode as supernovae, and the most massive collapse into black holes.
quizQuestion: What determines whether a star becomes a white dwarf, neutron star, or black hole?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, stellar evolution shows that mass is the primary determinant of a star's life and death. Stars below ~8 solar masses become white dwarfs; those between ~8-20 solar masses explode as supernovae leaving neutron stars; those above ~20-25 solar masses collapse into black holes.
wowFactor: A star's entire life story is written in its birth mass. Our Sun is too small to explode — it'll just quietly fade. But a star 25 times heavier will end in the most violent explosion in the universe: a supernova.

variants.question: True or false: A star's mass is the primary factor determining whether it becomes a white dwarf, neutron star, or black hole.
Stars above roughly ___ solar masses collapse into black holes after supernova.
What happens to stars between 8 and 20 solar masses after they explode as supernovae?
What single property of a star determines its ultimate fate as white dwarf, neutron star, or black hole?

visualDescription: Three stars in a row — a Sun-like star gently puffing off a planetary nebula, a supergiant exploding in a supernova, a massive star collapsing to a black hole — mass written in their destiny.

statement: Only three countries in the world have not officially adopted the metric system (SI): the United States, Myanmar, and Liberia.
quizQuestion: How many countries have NOT officially adopted the metric system?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the International System of Units (SI), the modern metric system, has been officially adopted by every country in the world except the United States, Myanmar (Burma), and Liberia. However, even these countries use metric in science and medicine. The US customary system is now technically defined in terms of SI units.
wowFactor: Only three countries haven't officially adopted metric — the US, Myanmar, and Liberia. The US still uses miles, pounds, and Fahrenheit. Ironically, even US science and medicine runs entirely on metric.

variants.question: True or false: Only three countries have not officially adopted the metric system.
The three countries that haven't officially adopted metric are the USA, Myanmar, and ___.
Despite not officially adopting metric, what fields in the US use metric measurements?
Which is the most notable major country that hasn't officially adopted the metric system?

visualDescription: A world map shows virtually every country colored green for metric adoption — except three tiny holdouts, one of them enormous: the United States, still measuring in miles and Fahrenheit.

statement: Cerium (atomic number 58) is the most abundant rare earth element — more common in Earth's crust than copper, tin, or lead, despite being called 'rare'.
quizQuestion: Which is the most abundant rare earth element?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, cerium (Ce, atomic number 58) is the most abundant of the lanthanide (rare earth) elements, comprising approximately 66 parts per million of Earth's crust — more than copper (50 ppm), tin (2 ppm), or lead (10 ppm). It is used in self-cleaning ovens, catalytic converters, and as a yellow glass colorant.
wowFactor: Cerium is more abundant than copper in Earth's crust — and it's called a 'rare earth'. It's used in self-cleaning ovens. Every time your oven cleans itself, cerium chemistry is burning away the grease.

variants.question: True or false: Cerium is more abundant in Earth's crust than copper.
Cerium has the atomic number ___.
What common household appliance uses cerium-based chemistry?
Which 'rare earth' element is actually the most abundant lanthanide, more common than copper?

visualDescription: A modern self-cleaning oven interior gleams after automatic cleaning — inside its heating elements, cerium chemistry has been burning away grease, the most abundant 'rare' earth quietly at work.

statement: Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935 — the same year he invented his famous cat thought experiment — and shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac.
quizQuestion: What paradox-revealing thought experiment did Schrödinger devise in 1935?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Schrödinger devised his cat thought experiment in 1935 to expose the absurdity of quantum superposition applied to everyday objects. That same year he coined 'quantum entanglement'. He had previously shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac.
wowFactor: Schrödinger created both quantum entanglement AND the dead/alive cat in the same year — and he actually invented the cat to argue AGAINST quantum mechanics, not celebrate it!

variants.question: True or false: Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935.
What year did Schrödinger win the Nobel Prize in Physics?
Which physicist coined 'quantum entanglement' and devised the famous cat thought experiment?
Schrödinger shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul ___.

visualDescription: A sealed steel box sits in a quantum physics laboratory, a semi-transparent cat simultaneously visible and not inside, probability waves rippling outward.

statement: Antimony's chemical symbol Sb comes from the Latin 'stibium', and its compounds were powdered as kohl eyeliner cosmetics in the ancient world.
quizQuestion: What Latin word gives antimony its chemical symbol 'Sb'?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, antimony (atomic number 51) has the symbol Sb from the Latin 'stibium'. Its compounds have been known since ancient times, powdered as kohl cosmetics, often known by the Arabic name 'kohl'. China is the largest producer.
wowFactor: The eyeliner worn in ancient Egypt was powdered antimony — a toxic heavy metal used as a cosmetic for thousands of years!

variants.question: True or false: antimony's symbol Sb comes from the Latin word 'stibium'.
What is antimony's atomic number?
What is antimony's chemical symbol?
Antimony compounds were used as ancient cosmetics, often known by the Arabic name ___.

visualDescription: An ancient Egyptian applies dark kohl eyeliner in a bronze mirror — the mineral in the cosmetic pot glints the same grey-silver as modern antimony ore.

statement: Hafnium is chemically so similar to zirconium that its existence was predicted in 1869 but not actually discovered until 1922 — 53 years of hiding in plain sight.
quizQuestion: How many years after Mendeleev predicted hafnium's existence was it actually discovered?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Dmitri Mendeleev predicted hafnium's existence in 1869, but it wasn't identified until 1922 by Dirk Coster and George de Hevesy — a 53-year gap. Hafnium is named after Hafnia, the Latin name for Copenhagen where it was finally identified.
wowFactor: Hafnium hid inside zirconium minerals for 53 years after scientists predicted it must exist — the two elements are so chemically alike they're nearly impossible to separate!

variants.question: True or false: hafnium was discovered before Mendeleev created the periodic table.
What year was hafnium finally discovered?
What city gave hafnium its name (from its Latin name Hafnia)?
Hafnium is named after Hafnia, the Latin name for ___, where it was discovered.

visualDescription: Two near-identical silvery metal samples sit side by side in a laboratory — zirconium and hafnium — labelled with atomic numbers 40 and 72.

statement: Tellurium exposure causes a garlic-like odour in the breath because the body converts it into dimethyl telluride gas, a volatile compound.
quizQuestion: What distinctive smell does tellurium exposure cause on the breath?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, tellurium is partly metabolized into dimethyl telluride, (CH₃)₂Te, a gas with a garlic-like odour exhaled in the breath of victims of tellurium poisoning. Tellurium's extreme rarity on Earth is partly because it was lost to space as a volatile hydride during Earth's formation.
wowFactor: Tellurium poisoning turns your breath into permanent garlic breath — early chemists who handled it were immediately identified by smell!

variants.question: True or false: tellurium exposure causes garlic-like breath odour.
What compound gives tellurium-exposed people garlic breath?
What is tellurium's atomic number?
Tellurium is metabolized into dimethyl telluride, a gas with a ___ odour exhaled in breath.

visualDescription: A Victorian chemist recoils from a silver-white sample, wafting his hand under his nose — a faint garlic cloud visible in the air around the tellurium specimen.

statement: Thallium became known as 'the poisoner's poison' because soluble thallium salts are nearly tasteless yet highly toxic, and poisoning causes distinctive hair loss.
quizQuestion: What sinister nickname did thallium earn because its salts are nearly tasteless yet lethal?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, thallium gained notoriety as 'the poisoner's poison' and 'inheritance powder' because its soluble salts are nearly tasteless, highly toxic, and poisoning typically causes distinctive hair loss. Its use in rat poisons and insecticides has been banned or restricted in many countries.
wowFactor: Thallium was the perfect murder weapon — nearly tasteless, causes hair loss that could be blamed on illness, and earned the name 'the poisoner's poison'!

variants.question: True or false: thallium poisoning characteristically causes hair loss.
What is thallium's atomic number?
What distinctive symptom typically results from thallium poisoning?
Thallium is known as 'the poisoner's poison' and 'inheritance ___' due to its near-tasteless toxicity.

sensitivityNote: Poison chemistry discussed in historical/scientific context.
visualDescription: A Victorian-era apothecary jar labelled in faded script sits on a shelf, a grey powder inside — a 19th-century crime scene sketch on the wall beside it.

statement: The neutron was discovered by James Chadwick in 1932, and within 13 years this single discovery had led to nuclear fission, the first nuclear reactor, and the first atomic bomb.
quizQuestion: How many years after Chadwick discovered the neutron was the first nuclear weapon detonated?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, James Chadwick discovered the neutron in 1932. This discovery led to nuclear fission in 1938, the first self-sustaining nuclear reactor (Chicago Pile-1, 1942), and Trinity — the first nuclear weapons test — in 1945. A 13-year chain from discovery to bomb.
wowFactor: Chadwick discovered the neutron in 1932 — just 13 years later humanity had nuclear bombs. One particle changed everything in little over a decade!

variants.question: True or false: the neutron was discovered by James Chadwick in 1932.
What year did James Chadwick discover the neutron?
What is the approximate mean lifetime of a free neutron outside an atomic nucleus?
The neutron was discovered by James ___ in 1932 at the Cavendish Laboratory.

sensitivityNote: Nuclear weapons mentioned in historical scientific context.
visualDescription: Chadwick at a Cavendish Laboratory bench in 1932 examines a cloud chamber track — a tiny smudge of white vapor that will change the world.

statement: Neptunium (atomic number 93) was the first transuranic element ever synthesized, created at Berkeley in 1940 and named after Neptune — because uranium is named after Uranus.
quizQuestion: Which element, named after the planet beyond Uranus, was the first transuranic element ever synthesized?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, neptunium (Np, atomic number 93) was first synthesized by Edwin McMillan and Philip H. Abelson at the Berkeley Radiation Laboratory in 1940. It is named after Neptune because uranium is named after Uranus — the solar system sequence maps to the element sequence.
wowFactor: Neptunium was named after Neptune because uranium is named after Uranus — scientists followed the solar system pattern when creating their own new elements!

variants.question: True or false: neptunium is the first element beyond uranium (atomic number 92).
What is neptunium's atomic number?
What year was neptunium first synthesized at Berkeley?
Neptunium is named after Neptune because uranium is named after ___, the next planet inward.

visualDescription: A Berkeley laboratory in 1940 — glowing radioactive samples in lead-lined containers, the periodic table on the wall ending at uranium, waiting for its first new entry.

statement: Rhenium, with an average concentration of just 1 part per billion in Earth's crust, is one of the rarest elements — yet it is inside the turbine blades of nearly every jet aircraft.
quizQuestion: What is rhenium's average concentration in Earth's crust?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, rhenium has an estimated average concentration of just 1 part per billion (ppb) in Earth's crust, making it one of the rarest stable elements. Nickel-based superalloys of rhenium are used in combustion chambers and turbine blades of jet engines — the largest single use for the element.
wowFactor: Rhenium is so rare it appears just once in every billion atoms of Earth's crust — yet it's inside the turbine blades of nearly every commercial aircraft flying today!

variants.question: True or false: rhenium is primarily used in jet engine turbine blades.
What is rhenium's atomic number?
What river gave rhenium its name?
Rhenium alloys containing up to 6% rhenium are used in jet engine ___ blades.

visualDescription: A jet engine turbine blade glows orange-red after a test run, cross-sectioned to show the exotic superalloy crystals — including the rare rhenium — inside.

statement: Francis Crick coined the 'central dogma' of molecular biology — information flows from DNA to RNA to protein and never backwards — and co-discovered DNA's double helix structure in 1953.
quizQuestion: What principle did Francis Crick term the 'central dogma' of molecular biology?
distractors: All life uses the same DNA
acceptableAnswers: information flows from dna to rna to protein
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Crick used the term 'central dogma' to describe the irreversible flow of genetic information: DNA makes RNA, RNA makes protein, and protein information cannot flow back to nucleic acids. Together with Watson, Wilkins, and Franklin, he deciphered DNA's helical structure in 1953.
wowFactor: Crick called it the 'central dogma' — the idea that DNA's instructions only flow one way, never backwards — and this one-way street runs every living cell on Earth!

variants.question: True or false: Crick's central dogma states that information can flow from protein back to DNA.
What year did Crick and Watson publish their DNA structure paper in Nature?
What Nobel Prize did Crick share in 1962?
Crick's 'central dogma' describes the flow of information: DNA → ___ → protein.

visualDescription: Crick and Watson stand beside a towering DNA double-helix model in Cambridge, 1953, the iconic spiral structure gleaming in the laboratory light.

statement: Neptune was the first planet discovered through mathematical prediction — astronomers calculated where it must be before anyone looked through a telescope.
quizQuestion: How was Neptune's location determined before it was first observed through a telescope?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Neptune was not discovered through direct observation but through mathematical prediction. Anomalies in Uranus's orbit led to independent calculations by John Couch Adams and Urbain Le Verrier, which guided Johann Gottfried Galle to observe it on September 23, 1846.
wowFactor: Neptune was found by math, not a telescope — two astronomers predicted exactly where an unknown planet must be, and it was right there!

variants.question: True or false: Neptune was discovered by accident while scanning the sky.
What year was Neptune first directly observed through a telescope?
What is Neptune's orbital period around the Sun?
Neptune was discovered when anomalies in ___ orbit led astronomers to calculate its position.

visualDescription: A 19th-century astronomer at a Berlin observatory peers through an eyepiece, marking a blue dot on a star chart — the predicted spot for an unseen world.

statement: Most of Earth's volcanoes are found underwater, because most of Earth's plate boundaries — where volcanoes form — lie beneath the ocean floor.
quizQuestion: Where are most of Earth's volcanoes actually located?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, most of Earth's plate boundaries are underwater, and since volcanoes most often form at plate boundaries, most volcanoes are found on the ocean floor. The famous Ring of Fire represents only a fraction of global volcanic activity.
wowFactor: The Ring of Fire gets all the fame, but most of Earth's volcanoes are hidden underwater — there's more volcanic activity on the ocean floor than on all continents combined!

variants.question: True or false: most of Earth's volcanoes are located underwater.
What tectonic boundary type causes most explosive volcano eruptions?
Volcanoes on the Pacific ___ of Fire are caused by convergent tectonic plates.
A scientist maps all Earth's volcanoes. Where does she find the majority?

visualDescription: A cross-section of the ocean floor shows dozens of volcanic cones erupting in the darkness below, glowing lava flows visible through the deep blue water.

statement: Coral reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor but support roughly 25% of all marine species — making them the ocean's most biodiverse ecosystems.
quizQuestion: What fraction of all marine species do coral reefs support despite covering less than 1% of the ocean floor?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, coral reefs are among the most diverse and productive ecosystems on Earth. Despite covering less than 1% of the ocean floor, they are home to approximately 25% of all marine species, giving them an ecological importance far beyond their physical size.
wowFactor: Coral reefs cover barely 1% of the ocean but house 25% of all sea life — they're the ocean's version of a city: tiny footprint, enormous population!

variants.question: True or false: coral reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor.
Coral reefs cover less than 1% of the ocean floor. What percentage of marine species do they support?

Coral reefs are sometimes called the ___ of the sea because of their extraordinary biodiversity.

visualDescription: An explosion of tropical colour underwater — thousands of fish species darting between coral formations, a miniature city of life on the ocean floor.

statement: A ruby is red because of chromium atoms in its corundum crystal structure — without chromium, the same mineral becomes a sapphire.
quizQuestion: What element gives ruby its red color?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the color of a ruby is due to the presence of chromium. Ruby is a variety of the mineral corundum (aluminium oxide) — and other varieties of gem-quality corundum are called sapphires. The same mineral, just with different impurities.
wowFactor: Ruby and sapphire are the SAME mineral — just different impurities! Chromium makes it red (ruby), titanium and iron make it blue (sapphire).

variants.question: True or false: ruby and sapphire are varieties of the same mineral, corundum.
What is the base mineral that makes up both ruby and sapphire?
Ruby scores ___ on the Mohs hardness scale, making it one of the hardest gems.

visualDescription: A side-by-side comparison: a glowing red ruby beside a blue sapphire, both on the same corundum crystal lattice diagram showing chromium atoms highlighted in the ruby's structure.

statement: Plate tectonics was not widely accepted by scientists until the 1960s, despite evidence dating back to Alfred Wegener's 1912 proposal of continental drift — 50 years of being mocked.
quizQuestion: In what decade did plate tectonics become widely accepted by scientists?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Alfred Wegener proposed continental drift in 1912 but was largely ridiculed. Plate tectonics only became the consensus geological theory in the 1960s when seafloor spreading and ocean floor magnetism provided definitive evidence.
wowFactor: Scientists mocked Wegener for 50 years for saying continents move — he was proven completely right, just decades after his death!

variants.question: True or false: Alfred Wegener proposed continental drift in 1912.
Who first proposed that continents drift apart in 1912?
What process of ocean floor creation provided key evidence for plate tectonics?
Plate tectonics became the accepted geological theory in the ___, about 50 years after Wegener's proposal.

visualDescription: A 1912 map of Earth with dashed lines showing the Pangaea puzzle-fit between continents — Wegener's radical idea mocked for 50 years before being proven right.

statement: Erwin Schrödinger devised the famous 'Schrödinger's Cat' thought experiment in 1935 to illustrate the paradox of quantum superposition.
quizQuestion: What did Schrödinger's 1935 thought experiment use to illustrate quantum superposition?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Schrödinger proposed the thought experiment in 1935 as a critique of the Copenhagen interpretation — showing the absurdity of applying quantum superposition to everyday objects like a cat in a box.
wowFactor: Schrödinger invented an imaginary cat that is simultaneously dead and alive — not to prove it, but to show how absurd quantum mechanics gets when applied to everyday objects!

variants.question: True or false: Schrödinger's Cat was a real experiment conducted in a laboratory.
Schrödinger's Cat thought experiment was proposed to critique which quantum theory interpretation?
Schrödinger's 1935 thought experiment shows what happens when quantum superposition is applied to everyday life. What's simultaneously dead and alive?
Schrödinger's Cat was proposed in ___ to critique quantum theory.

visualDescription: Pixel-art steel box with a question mark, half a cat visible — one half alive and tail wagging, one half skeleton — quantum physics made adorably weird.

statement: Schrödinger derived his wave equation during a Christmas holiday in 1925–26, reportedly inspired by a romantic retreat in the Alps with a mysterious companion.
quizQuestion: Where was Schrödinger when he derived his famous wave equation?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Schrödinger developed the Schrödinger equation during Christmas 1925–26 at a mountain villa in Arosa, Switzerland — one of science's most productive holidays.
wowFactor: Schrödinger derived the equation that defines quantum mechanics while on a Christmas ski holiday with a mystery woman in the Swiss Alps — science's most romantic breakthrough!

visualDescription: Pixel-art snow-covered Alpine chalet at Christmas 1926, Schrödinger at a desk writing equations, mountains glowing outside, physics emerging from holiday inspiration.

statement: Wilhelm Röntgen discovered X-rays on 8 November 1895 and named them 'X-rays' because their nature was then unknown — 'X' for the unknown.
quizQuestion: Why did Röntgen name his 1895 discovery 'X-rays'?
distractors: They moved in a cross pattern
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Röntgen named the rays 'X-rays' (using 'X' as the mathematical symbol for the unknown) because he could not identify what type of radiation they were.
wowFactor: Röntgen was so puzzled by his discovery he called them 'X-rays' — 'X' for the unknown — and the name stuck forever even after we learned exactly what they are!

variants.question: True or false: Röntgen named X-rays because their nature was unknown at the time of discovery.

visualDescription: Pixel-art 1895 lab with Röntgen pointing at a glowing hand X-ray on the wall, a question mark hovering over the rays, his name on the Nobel Prize certificate beside it.

statement: Plate tectonics explains how continents drift — and all Earth's continents were once a single supercontinent called Pangaea, which began breaking up ~175 million years ago.
quizQuestion: What was the name of the supercontinent that contained all of Earth's land mass before it broke up?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Pangaea formed about 335 million years ago and began breaking up around 175 million years ago, eventually forming the seven modern continents through plate tectonic movement.
wowFactor: Every continent on Earth was once jammed together in one giant land mass — Pangaea — and it's still splitting apart today at about 2.5 cm per year!

variants.question: True or false: All Earth's continents were once part of a supercontinent called Pangaea.

visualDescription: Pixel-art Earth 175 million years ago with Pangaea intact, then a time-lapse split showing the continents slowly drifting apart to modern positions.

statement: Tectonic plates move at roughly the same speed as a human fingernail grows — about 2–5 cm per year.
quizQuestion: How fast do tectonic plates move, approximately?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, tectonic plates move at speeds of 2–5 cm per year on average — equivalent to the growth rate of a human fingernail.
wowFactor: The continents are moving right now — about as fast as your fingernails grow. In 250 million years, they'll form a new supercontinent called Amasia!

variants.question: True or false: Tectonic plates move at roughly the same speed as human fingernail growth.

statement: Glaciers and ice sheets hold about 69% of all freshwater on Earth — and if all of it melted, sea levels would rise by approximately 65 metres.
quizQuestion: What percentage of Earth's freshwater is stored in glaciers and ice sheets?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, glaciers and ice caps contain approximately 69% of Earth's freshwater, with Antarctica alone holding about 61% — and complete melting would raise sea levels ~65 m.
wowFactor: Two-thirds of all the fresh water on Earth is locked in ice — if all glaciers melted, every coastal city on Earth would be underwater!

statement: Granite is one of Earth's most common rocks and can be billions of years old — the Jack Hills zircon crystals in Western Australia date to 4.4 billion years ago.
quizQuestion: Zircon crystals from Western Australia are the oldest known materials on Earth. How old are they?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, the Acasta Gneiss in Canada (4.03 billion years old) and Jack Hills zircon crystals (4.4 billion years old) are among the oldest known geological materials on Earth.
wowFactor: Some rocks in Western Australia are 4.4 billion years old — nearly as old as the Earth itself, which formed 4.5 billion years ago!

variants.question: True or false: The Jack Hills zircon crystals are older than the Earth.

statement: Amber is fossilized tree resin that can preserve insects, plants, and even feathered dinosaur tails perfectly for millions of years.
quizQuestion: What material is amber made from?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, amber forms when tree resin hardens over millions of years, and can preserve biological material in extraordinary detail — including 99 million-year-old feathered dinosaur tails discovered in 2016.
wowFactor: Amber can trap an insect perfectly for 100 million years — Jurassic Park wasn't entirely wrong about ancient DNA being preserved in amber!

statement: Lava can reach temperatures of up to 1,250°C — hot enough to melt most metals and vaporize water instantly on contact.
quizQuestion: What is the maximum temperature that lava can reach?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, lava temperatures range from about 700°C to 1,250°C depending on composition, with mafic (basaltic) lavas being the hottest.
wowFactor: Lava is so hot it would melt copper, gold, and silver on contact — at 1,250°C it's nearly twice as hot as your household oven's maximum temperature!

statement: The Great Barrier Reef is the world's largest coral reef system, stretching 2,300 km along Australia's northeastern coast and visible from space.
quizQuestion: How long is the Great Barrier Reef?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, the Great Barrier Reef stretches approximately 2,300 km along Queensland's coast and covers an area of 344,400 km² — large enough to be seen from space.
wowFactor: The Great Barrier Reef is so large it's visible from space — 2,300 km of living coral built by tiny polyps over millions of years!

statement: Coral bleaching occurs when water is too warm — corals expel their symbiotic algae, turning white, and can die within weeks without them.
quizQuestion: What causes coral bleaching?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, coral bleaching is triggered when ocean temperatures rise by just 1–2°C above the usual maximum, causing corals to expel the symbiotic zooxanthellae algae that give them colour and nutrition.
wowFactor: A coral reef can turn completely white and die if the ocean warms by just 1°C above normal — coral bleaching is climate change's most visible victim!

variants.question: True or false: Coral bleaching is triggered by a temperature rise of just 1–2°C above the seasonal maximum.

visualDescription: Pixel-art before-and-after reef: left panel vivid orange-purple living coral, right panel ghost-white bleached coral, a thermometer showing +1.5°C above.

statement: It takes approximately 500 to 1,000 years for nature to form just 2.5 cm (one inch) of topsoil from bedrock.
quizQuestion: How many years does it take to form 2.5 cm of topsoil naturally?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, soil formation (pedogenesis) is extremely slow — forming 1 inch (2.5 cm) of topsoil can take 500–1,000 years depending on climate, vegetation, and parent rock.
wowFactor: We're plowing through 2.5 cm of topsoil that took 1,000 years to form — and the world's topsoil is eroding 10–40 times faster than it's forming!

variants.question: True or false: Topsoil takes hundreds of years to form naturally.

statement: The Richter scale is logarithmic — each whole number increase represents 10 times greater ground motion and about 31.6 times more energy released.
quizQuestion: On the Richter scale, each whole number increase represents how many times greater ground motion?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, the Richter scale (and moment magnitude scale) is logarithmic, meaning a magnitude 7.0 earthquake has 10 times the ground motion of a magnitude 6.0, and releases about 31.6 times more energy.
wowFactor: A Richter 8 earthquake isn't twice as strong as a Richter 4 — it's 10,000 times stronger. Each step on the scale multiplies the shaking by 10!

variants.question: True or false: The Richter scale is a logarithmic scale.

statement: Basalt is the most abundant rock on Earth's surface — most of the ocean floor is made of basalt, covering about 70% of Earth's surface.
quizQuestion: What rock type makes up most of the ocean floor?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, basalt covers the entire ocean floor (about 70% of Earth's surface) and is the most voluminous rock type on Earth — it erupts from mid-ocean ridges continuously.
wowFactor: 70% of Earth's surface is ocean floor — and it's all made of basalt. The most common rock on our planet is the one we never see because it's underwater!

statement: The Grand Canyon was carved by the Colorado River over 5–6 million years, cutting through 1.8 billion-year-old rock to reach a depth of 1.6 km.
quizQuestion: How deep is the Grand Canyon?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, the Grand Canyon is ~1.6 km deep and ~446 km long, carved by the Colorado River over 5–6 million years through rock layers as old as 1.8 billion years.
wowFactor: The Colorado River spent 5 million years carving the Grand Canyon — patiently cutting 1.6 km into rock without stopping for a single day!

statement: Rubies and sapphires are actually the same mineral — corundum — with rubies getting their red colour from chromium and sapphires getting blue from iron and titanium.
quizQuestion: Rubies and sapphires are both varieties of which mineral?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, both rubies and sapphires are varieties of the mineral corundum (aluminium oxide, Al₂O₃) — the difference is their trace element content causing different colours.
wowFactor: A ruby and a sapphire are the same rock — just coloured differently! Swap the chromium for iron and titanium, and your red gemstone turns blue!

statement: Jade is actually two different minerals sold under the same name: nephrite and jadeite, with jadeite being rarer and more valuable.
quizQuestion: How many different minerals are sold as 'jade'?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, 'jade' refers to two distinct minerals — nephrite (a calcium-magnesium silicate) and jadeite (a sodium-aluminium silicate) — both prized in Chinese and Mesoamerican cultures.
wowFactor: When you buy 'jade', you might get one of two completely different minerals — nephrite or jadeite — and most people don't know there's a difference!

statement: Asbestos was called 'the magic mineral' in antiquity — ancient Greeks believed it came from the fur of a salamander that lived in fire.
quizQuestion: What did ancient Greeks believe asbestos fibre came from?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, ancient Greeks used 'asbestos' (meaning 'indestructible' in Greek) and believed the fireproof fibres came from the fur of a mythical salamander that could live in flames.
wowFactor: Ancient Greeks thought asbestos came from magic fire-dwelling salamanders — and wore it as tablecloths to impress guests by throwing them into fires without burning!

variants.question: True or false: Ancient Greeks believed asbestos came from a fire-dwelling salamander.
Asbestos means 'indestructible' in Greek. Why was it valued historically?
Ancient Greeks named this fireproof mineral 'indestructible' after the Greek word. What is it?
'Asbestos' means ___ in ancient Greek.

visualDescription: Pixel-art ancient Greek symposium with a toga-clad host holding an asbestos tablecloth over flames, guests astonished, a mythical salamander in a fire in the corner.

statement: Francis Crick and James Watson discovered the double-helix structure of DNA in 1953, building on crucial X-ray data produced by Rosalind Franklin.
quizQuestion: What crucial data did Rosalind Franklin provide that helped Crick and Watson discover DNA's structure?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Rosalind Franklin's X-ray diffraction images (especially Photo 51) provided critical evidence for the double-helix structure, though Franklin received little credit during her lifetime.
wowFactor: Watson and Crick solved DNA's structure using Rosalind Franklin's X-ray data — without her Photo 51, they might never have cracked it, yet she received no Nobel Prize!

variants.question: True or false: Rosalind Franklin's X-ray data was crucial to discovering DNA's structure.
Crick and Watson discovered DNA's double-helix structure in 1953. In which city?
Rosalind Franklin's famous 'Photo 51' was crucial evidence for discovering what?
Crick and Watson used Franklin's ___ crystallography images to discover DNA's structure.

visualDescription: Pixel-art 1953 Cambridge lab with Crick and Watson building a double-helix model, Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51 X-ray image pinned to the wall behind them.

statement: Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51 X-ray image of DNA, taken in 1952, was shown to Watson without her knowledge and was the key to solving DNA's structure.
quizQuestion: Rosalind Franklin's Photo 51 was shown to Watson without her knowledge. What did it reveal?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Photo 51 clearly showed DNA's helical structure and its dimensions — when Watson saw it (shown by Franklin's colleague Maurice Wilkins without her permission), it was decisive in solving the DNA puzzle.
wowFactor: Rosalind Franklin took the photo that cracked the code of life — then watched Watson, Crick, and Wilkins win the Nobel Prize for her discovery, while she received no credit in her lifetime!

variants.question: True or false: Photo 51 was shown to Watson without Rosalind Franklin's knowledge.
Franklin's Photo 51 was shown to Watson by which colleague?
This 1952 X-ray image revealed DNA's helical structure and was taken without permission to Watson. Name it.
Rosalind Franklin's ___ 51 X-ray image was the key to solving DNA's structure.

visualDescription: Pixel-art Rosalind Franklin at her X-ray machine in 1952, Photo 51 emerging — a shadowy hand reaching for it without her knowledge, Nobel Prize medal in the distance.

statement: Every snowflake has a unique six-sided crystal structure — no two are identical because of the complex conditions each faces as it falls.
quizQuestion: How many sides does a snowflake crystal always have?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, snowflakes always have six-fold (hexagonal) symmetry because of how water molecules bond in ice — but the exact shape of each arm varies based on temperature and humidity encountered while falling.
wowFactor: Every snowflake has exactly 6 sides but is completely unique — the complex journey each crystal takes falling through different temperatures creates an infinite variety of six-sided shapes!

variants.question: True or false: All snowflakes have six-fold symmetry.
Snowflakes always have 6 sides due to how water molecules bond. What is 6-fold symmetry called?
Every snowflake has 6 sides but is unique. Why are they all six-sided?
variants.correctAnswer: Water molecule bonding creates hexagonal ice crystals

variants.question: Snowflakes always have ___ sides because of how water molecules bond in ice.

visualDescription: Pixel-art close-up of six unique snowflakes, each with six arms in different patterns, falling through frozen winter air, all perfectly hexagonal.

statement: A typical cumulus cloud weighs around 500 tonnes — yet it stays aloft because the tiny water droplets are spread over a huge volume of air.
quizQuestion: Approximately how heavy is a typical cumulus cloud?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, a cumulus cloud's water content totals about 500 metric tonnes — but this is dispersed in billions of tiny droplets spread across millions of cubic metres of air, making each droplet too light to fall.
wowFactor: A fluffy white cloud weighs 500 tonnes — heavier than a Boeing 747 fully loaded — yet floats because its water is spread across millions of cubic metres of air!

variants.question: True or false: A typical cumulus cloud weighs several hundred tonnes.
Clouds stay aloft despite their weight because their water is spread over a huge volume. What process forms clouds?
This weather phenomenon weighs ~500 tonnes but floats because water droplets are spread over a huge volume. What?
A typical cumulus cloud weighs about ___ tonnes.

visualDescription: Pixel-art fluffy white cumulus cloud with a scale showing 500 tonnes, next to a Boeing 747 also at 350 tonnes, both floating effortlessly in blue sky.

statement: If Earth's 4.5 billion year history were compressed into a 24-hour day, humans would appear only in the last 1.5 seconds before midnight.
quizQuestion: If Earth's history were one 24-hour day, when would humans appear?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Earth is approximately 4.54 billion years old; anatomically modern humans appeared ~300,000 years ago — representing less than 0.007% of Earth's history.
wowFactor: On the cosmic calendar of Earth's history, all of human civilization — every empire, every invention, every moment in history — happens in the last 1.5 seconds of a 24-hour day!

variants.question: True or false: Humans represent less than 0.01% of Earth's total history.
Earth is 4.5 billion years old. When did the dinosaurs appear on the 24-hour Earth day?
On a 24-hour Earth timeline, humans appear in the last 1.5 seconds. How old is Earth?
If Earth's history were 24 hours, humans would appear in the last ___ seconds.

visualDescription: Pixel-art 24-hour clock showing Earth's history, dinosaurs appearing at 11:49 PM, humans as a tiny sliver in the last 1.5 seconds before midnight.

statement: Organic chemistry studies carbon-containing compounds — and carbon can form over 10 million different known compounds, more than any other element.
quizQuestion: How many different known carbon compounds exist?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, organic chemistry (the chemistry of carbon-containing compounds) is vast because carbon forms stable bonds with itself and other elements in unlimited chain and ring combinations.
wowFactor: Carbon is the world's most versatile atom — it forms over 10 million known compounds, from diamonds to DNA, making it the backbone of all life and chemistry!

variants.question: True or false: Carbon forms more known compounds than any other element.
Organic chemistry studies carbon compounds. What are the other most common elements in organic molecules?
This element forms over 10 million known compounds — more than any other. Which element?
Organic chemistry studies compounds containing ___ — the most versatile element.

visualDescription: Pixel-art carbon atom at the centre of a web of 10 million+ branching molecular structures — DNA strands, diamonds, oil molecules, vitamins all radiating outward.

statement: Antimony compounds have been used as black eye makeup (kohl) since ancient Egypt, over 4,000 years ago.
quizQuestion: How long ago was antimony first used as a black eye makeup?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, antimony sulphide (stibnite, Sb₂S₃) was used as kohl eyeliner in ancient Egypt and Mesopotamia over 4,000 years ago — making it one of the oldest cosmetics known.
wowFactor: Ancient Egyptians wore toxic antimony on their eyes as makeup for 4,000 years — yet somehow it was also used medicinally and never killed them from cosmetic use!

variants.question: True or false: Antimony compounds were used as eye makeup in ancient Egypt.
Antimony compounds used as eye makeup are called kohl. What is the chemical symbol for antimony?
This element was used as black eye makeup in ancient Egypt over 4,000 years ago. Name it.
Antimony sulphide was used as ___ eye makeup in ancient Egypt over 4,000 years ago.

visualDescription: Pixel-art ancient Egyptian queen applying kohl eyeliner from a small antimony pot, the Nile in the background, 4,000 BCE date stamp on the cosmetic jar.

statement: Ethanol is the only type of alcohol safe to drink — methanol, which smells similar, causes blindness and death even in small doses.
quizQuestion: Which type of alcohol is safe to drink?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, ethanol (C₂H₅OH) is the only alcohol consumed safely, while methanol (CH₃OH) is metabolised to formaldehyde and formic acid — causing blindness from as little as 10 mL and death from 25+ mL.
wowFactor: Methanol and ethanol smell alike, but methanol causes permanent blindness in tiny amounts — one wrong letter (C₂ vs CH₃) separates a cocktail from a poison!

variants.question: True or false: Methanol and ethanol are both safe for human consumption.
Ethanol is safe to drink. What is the common name for methanol?
This alcohol type is safe to drink; the other common type causes blindness. Which is safe?
___ is the only alcohol safe to drink; methanol causes blindness.

visualDescription: Pixel-art chemistry lab with two identical-looking bottles: 'ETHANOL ✓' and 'METHANOL ✗', molecular formulas beneath, a skull-and-crossbones on the methanol.

statement: The Sun is about halfway through its 10-billion-year life and will eventually expand into a red giant, engulfing Mercury, Venus, and possibly Earth.
quizQuestion: When the Sun expands into a red giant, which inner planets will it likely engulf?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, in about 5 billion years the Sun will exhaust its hydrogen fuel and expand into a red giant, engulfing Mercury and Venus and possibly Earth's orbit.
wowFactor: The Sun will eventually eat Mercury and Venus — and maybe Earth too. Our star is halfway through its life and its grand finale involves swallowing the inner solar system!

variants.question: True or false: The Sun will expand into a red giant in about 5 billion years.
The Sun is a main-sequence star. What type of star will it become after the red giant phase?

The Sun will expand into a red giant in about ___ billion years.

visualDescription: Pixel-art solar system 5 billion years future: a bloated red giant Sun engulfing Mercury and Venus, Earth's scorched surface at the edge, a white dwarf at the end.

statement: The Solar System formed about 4.6 billion years ago from a collapsing cloud of gas and dust called a solar nebula.
quizQuestion: How long ago did the Solar System form?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, the Solar System formed approximately 4.568 billion years ago when a molecular cloud of gas and dust collapsed under its own gravity, forming the Sun and planets.
wowFactor: The Solar System is 4.6 billion years old — and humans have existed for only 0.006% of that time. We're the universe's newest cosmic experiment!

variants.question: True or false: The Solar System formed from a collapsing cloud of gas and dust.
The Solar System formed 4.6 billion years ago. What is the theory of solar system formation called?
Our Solar System formed 4.6 billion years ago from a collapsing cloud of gas and dust. What is this cloud called?
The Solar System formed about ___ billion years ago from a collapsing solar nebula.

visualDescription: Pixel-art time-lapse of a dust cloud collapsing into a disc, then a glowing Sun at the centre with planets forming from the rotating ring around it.

statement: Botanically, zucchini (courgette) is a fruit — the mature fruit of a flowering vine — though it is almost universally treated as a vegetable in cooking.
quizQuestion: Is zucchini (courgette) botanically a fruit or a vegetable?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, zucchini is botanically a fruit (the mature ovary of a flowering plant) but is used as a vegetable in culinary contexts — one of many foods classified differently in botany vs. cooking.
wowFactor: Zucchini is a fruit! Not a vegetable. Just like tomatoes, cucumbers, and avocados — botany classifies anything with seeds as a fruit, regardless of how you cook it!

variants.question: True or false: Zucchini is botanically classified as a fruit.
Zucchini is the fruit of which plant family?
This green 'vegetable' is botanically a fruit — it's the mature ovary of a flowering vine. Name it.
Zucchini is botanically a ___, not a vegetable, because it contains seeds.

visualDescription: Pixel-art zucchini with a 'FRUIT' badge, surrounded by tomatoes, cucumbers, and avocados also wearing 'FRUIT' badges, confused cooks in the background.

statement: Some cypress trees (Cupressus) can live for thousands of years — the Sarv-e Abarkuh cypress in Iran is estimated to be 4,000–5,000 years old.
quizQuestion: Approximately how old is the Sarv-e Abarkuh cypress tree in Iran?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, the Sarv-e Abarkuh (Zoroastrian Sarv) in Iran is one of the world's oldest living organisms, estimated at 4,000–5,000 years old, predating the Roman Empire by over 3,000 years.
wowFactor: A single cypress tree in Iran is so old it was already ancient when Rome was founded — the Sarv-e Abarkuh has been alive for 4,000–5,000 years!

variants.question: True or false: The Sarv-e Abarkuh cypress is one of the world's oldest living trees.
The Sarv-e Abarkuh cypress is in which country?

The Sarv-e Abarkuh cypress in Iran is estimated to be ___ years old.

visualDescription: Pixel-art ancient gnarled cypress towering over an Iranian village, a timeline showing the Roman Empire, Egypt's Middle Kingdom, and the Bronze Age all within its lifetime.

statement: Earth's mantle makes up about 84% of Earth's total volume and is made mostly of silicate rock that flows extremely slowly like a very thick liquid.
quizQuestion: What percentage of Earth's total volume is the mantle?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Earth's mantle extends from the base of the crust to a depth of ~2,900 km, comprising about 84% of Earth's total volume and consisting mainly of silicate minerals.
wowFactor: Earth's mantle is 84% of the planet's volume — and it's not solid or liquid, but a super-viscous rock that flows like warm tar over millions of years!

variants.question: True or false: Earth's mantle makes up more than 80% of Earth's total volume.

Earth's mantle makes up about ___% of Earth's total volume.

visualDescription: Pixel-art Earth cross-section with the massive mantle layer glowing orange, labeled '84% of Earth's volume', thin crust above, core below.

statement: Talc is the softest known mineral, rating 1 on the Mohs hardness scale — it can be scratched with a fingernail.
quizQuestion: What is the softest known mineral, rated 1 on the Mohs hardness scale?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, talc is the reference mineral for 1 on the Mohs scale of hardness — the scale's softest point — meaning any fingernail can scratch it.
wowFactor: Talc is so soft a baby could scratch it with a fingernail — and yet it's used in baby powder, paper, and even the world's largest underground mine!

variants.question: True or false: Talc is the softest mineral on the Mohs hardness scale.
Talc rates 1 on the Mohs scale. Diamond rates 10. What rates 10?

Talc rates ___ on the Mohs hardness scale — the softest possible.

visualDescription: Pixel-art Mohs hardness scale from 1 to 10: talc at the bottom with a fingernail scratching it, diamond at the top cutting glass, minerals in between.

statement: Gypsum is the mineral used to make plaster of Paris — and the White Sands desert in New Mexico is made almost entirely of gypsum crystals.
quizQuestion: What mineral makes up the White Sands desert in New Mexico?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, the White Sands National Park in New Mexico contains the world's largest gypsum dunefield, with brilliant white gypsum sand covering 710 km².
wowFactor: The world's whitest desert isn't quartz sand — White Sands, New Mexico is made of gypsum crystals, the same mineral in your school's chalk and hospital plaster casts!

variants.question: True or false: The White Sands desert in New Mexico is made of gypsum crystals.
Gypsum is used to make plaster of Paris. What is plaster of Paris chemically?

White Sands National Park is made almost entirely of ___ crystals.

visualDescription: Pixel-art White Sands National Park: blindingly white dunes stretching to the horizon, a hand picking up a gypsum crystal, 'Made of Gypsum!' label in the sky.

statement: Jordan's ancient city of Petra was carved directly into rose-red sandstone cliffs over 2,000 years ago by the Nabataean people.
quizQuestion: The ancient city of Petra in Jordan was carved into which type of rock?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Petra's famous architecture — including the Treasury (Al-Khazneh) — was carved from the distinctive rose-red Nubian sandstone cliffs by the Nabataeans from around the 4th century BCE.
wowFactor: The entire city of Petra was carved from solid rock — the Nabataeans cut palaces, temples, and tombs directly into rose-red sandstone cliffs 2,000 years ago!

variants.question: True or false: Petra was carved from sandstone cliffs over 2,000 years ago.
Petra is in which modern country?

visualDescription: Pixel-art the Treasury at Petra carved from rose-red sandstone, Nabataean figures etched in the facade, the Siq gorge entrance at the side.

statement: Feldspar is the most abundant mineral group in Earth's crust, making up about 60% of all crustal rocks.
quizQuestion: What percentage of Earth's crust is made up of feldspar minerals?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, feldspars are aluminosilicate minerals that make up approximately 60% of Earth's crust by volume, found in igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks.
wowFactor: 60% of the ground under your feet is made of one mineral family — feldspar. It's the most common rock-forming mineral on Earth!

variants.question: True or false: Feldspar is the most abundant mineral group in Earth's crust.

statement: The Yellowstone supervolcano sits above a magma hotspot — and if it erupted, the ash cloud could cover most of North America.
quizQuestion: If Yellowstone's supervolcano erupted, what would its ash cloud potentially cover?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Yellowstone's last major eruption 640,000 years ago deposited ash across most of North America — a future eruption of similar scale would be a global catastrophe.
wowFactor: Yellowstone's supervolcano hasn't erupted in 640,000 years — and when it does, the ash cloud could bury most of North America under meters of volcanic dust!

variants.question: True or false: Yellowstone sits above a volcanic hotspot.

statement: The 'War of Currents' in the 1880s–90s was a battle between Tesla's AC (alternating current) and Edison's DC (direct current) — AC won and powers the world today.
quizQuestion: In the 'War of Currents', whose electrical system won and now powers the world?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, the War of Currents in the 1880s–90s saw Edison promoting DC and Westinghouse/Tesla promoting AC — AC won because it can transmit electricity over long distances far more efficiently.
wowFactor: Nikola Tesla beat Thomas Edison in history's most electrifying corporate battle — AC current won the 'War of Currents' and now powers literally everything!

variants.question: True or false: AC (alternating current) powers most of the world's electrical grid.

The 'War of Currents' was between Edison's ___ and Tesla's alternating current.

visualDescription: Pixel-art 1890s battle: Tesla in one corner with AC lightning bolts, Edison with DC batteries in the other, Westinghouse referee, the world's power grid at stake.

statement: Obsidian, a volcanic glass, can be fractured to produce edges sharper than surgical steel — ancient peoples used it for cutting tools and blades.
quizQuestion: Obsidian blades can be sharper than surgical steel. What type of rock is obsidian?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, obsidian is a naturally occurring volcanic glass formed from rapidly cooling lava, and obsidian scalpels can achieve edges thinner than 3 nanometres — potentially sharper than surgical steel.
wowFactor: Ancient humans made surgical-quality scalpels from a volcanic rock — obsidian blades can be sharper than stainless steel and were used for cutting tools 700,000 years ago!

variants.question: True or false: Obsidian blades can be sharper than surgical steel.
Obsidian forms when lava cools too rapidly to crystallise. What mineral group is it composed of?

Obsidian is a volcanic ___ that can be fractured sharper than surgical steel.

visualDescription: Pixel-art obsidian blade beside a surgical scalpel, both on a magnified background showing the obsidian's 3-nanometre edge — sharper than steel.

statement: Wolves reintroduced to Yellowstone in 1995 changed the course of rivers — their presence altered deer behaviour, allowing riverside vegetation to recover.
quizQuestion: Wolves reintroduced to Yellowstone in 1995 changed what unexpected aspect of the park?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, wolves returning to Yellowstone reduced elk grazing near rivers, allowing vegetation to recover, which stabilized riverbanks — a trophic cascade that literally changed river courses.
wowFactor: Wolves changed rivers — their return to Yellowstone in 1995 set off a chain reaction that stabilized riverbanks and shifted river courses. This is called a trophic cascade!

variants.question: True or false: Wolves were reintroduced to Yellowstone National Park in 1995.
The change wolves caused to Yellowstone's rivers is an example of what ecological phenomenon?
This apex predator's 1995 reintroduction to Yellowstone unexpectedly changed river courses through a chain reaction. Name it.
Wolves reintroduced to Yellowstone in ___ changed the course of rivers through a trophic cascade.

visualDescription: Pixel-art Yellowstone timeline: 1994 bare river banks with deer grazing, 1995 wolves arrive, 2000 lush vegetation returning, rivers stabilizing and shifting.

statement: The Law of Superposition states that in undisturbed rock layers, older rocks are at the bottom and newer rocks are at the top — the foundation of geological dating.
quizQuestion: In undisturbed rock strata, where are the oldest rocks found?
explanation: Per Wikipedia, Nicolas Steno's Law of Superposition (1669) states that in any undeformed sequence of sedimentary rock layers, the oldest layer is at the bottom and the youngest at the top.
wowFactor: Rock layers are like a geological calendar — the deeper you dig, the further back in time you go. The Law of Superposition turns a cliff face into a time machine!

variants.question: True or false: In undisturbed rock strata, the oldest layers are at the bottom.
Nicolas Steno formulated the Law of Superposition in 1669. What was his nationality?
This geological law says older rock layers are at the bottom. Name it.
The Law of Superposition says older rocks are at the ___ in undisturbed strata.

visualDescription: Pixel-art cliff face with distinct rock layers labeled from 'oldest' at the bottom to 'newest' at the top, a geologist with a hammer pointing at the sequence.

statement: Erwin Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935, the same year he devised his famous cat thought experiment.
quizQuestion: Who coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, Schrödinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935 — the same year he published the famous Schrödinger's cat thought experiment. He shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac for 'the discovery of new productive forms of atomic theory.'
wowFactor: The man who invented 'Schrödinger's cat' also coined the phrase 'quantum entanglement' — two of the most mind-bending concepts in physics, both in the same year!

variants.question: True or false: Schrödinger shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac.
Schrödinger's most famous equation provides a way to calculate the ___ function of a quantum system.
A physics student learns that the phrase 'quantum entanglement' was coined in 1935 by the same person who devised a famous cat paradox. Who?

visualDescription: A thoughtful physicist at a chalkboard in 1935 Vienna, a cartoon cat in a superposed state of alive and dead sketched alongside his equations.

statement: Schrödinger developed the Schrödinger equation, which provides a way to calculate how quantum wave functions evolve over time.
quizQuestion: What equation did Schrödinger develop to describe how quantum systems evolve?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Schrödinger is recognized for devising the Schrödinger equation — an equation that provides a way to calculate the wave function of a system and how it changes dynamically in time. It is one of the fundamental equations of quantum mechanics.
wowFactor: Before Schrödinger's equation, physicists had no tool to predict how quantum systems would behave over time — his equation gave the first mathematical 'movie' of the quantum world!

variants.question: True or false: the Schrödinger equation describes how quantum wave functions evolve over time.
Schrödinger died of ___ in Vienna in 1961.

visualDescription: A wave function plotted in brilliant colours on a chalkboard, oscillating like a ghostly wave across a quantum probability landscape.

statement: Wilhelm Röntgen became the first ever recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901, awarded for his discovery of X-rays.
quizQuestion: Who was the first recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics, in 1901?
explanation: According to Wikipedia and Wikidata, Wilhelm Conrad Röntgen became the first recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901 — awarded 'in recognition of the extraordinary services he has rendered by the discovery of the remarkable rays subsequently named after him,' i.e., X-rays.
wowFactor: The very first Nobel Prize in Physics ever awarded went to the man who discovered X-rays — a discovery so astonishing that people initially called them 'ghostly rays' and thought he had gone mad!

variants.question: True or false: The first Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded in 1901 to Röntgen for discovering X-rays.
The chemical element ___ was named in Röntgen's honour.

Which physicist discovered X-rays and won the first Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901?

visualDescription: An X-ray image of a human hand floating above an antique laboratory apparatus, ghostly bones visible against a dark background, circa 1895.

statement: Wilhelm Röntgen refused to patent his X-ray discovery and gave his Nobel Prize money to the University of Würzburg, believing science should be freely shared.
quizQuestion: What did Röntgen do with his Nobel Prize money?
distractors: Returned it to the Nobel Foundation
Used it to fund more experiments
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Röntgen refused to take out patents on X-rays because he wanted his discovery to benefit humanity freely. He donated his Nobel Prize money to the University of Würzburg. He also died in relative poverty after his savings were wiped out by post-WWI inflation.
wowFactor: Röntgen invented the technology that revolutionised medicine, refused to patent it so the world could use it for free, and died poor because he gave his Nobel Prize money away!

variants.question: True or false: Röntgen refused to patent his X-ray discovery.
Röntgen discovered X-rays in ___.
